  • On a 1000px viewport: 2.5vw = 25px (≈1.5625rem) → within range → renders at ~1.5rem
  • On a 1600px viewport: 2.5vw = 40px (2.5rem) → at maximum → renders at 2.5rem

Viewport-relative unit: vw (viewport width) is most commonly used as the preferred value because it scales linearly with screen width, creating truly fluid typography without any media query breakpoints.

SECTION: Rounded Corners & Box-Sizing

Question 47 — Code Output Prediction

A developer wants a pill-shaped button. They write:

.pill {
  width: 160px;
  height: 40px;
  border-radius: 999px;
  box-sizing: border-box;
  border: 2px solid black;
  padding: 0 16px;
}

Questions: (a) What is the inner content area's width? (b) Would border-radius: 50% produce the same pill shape? (c) Why use border-radius: 999px instead of border-radius: 20px for a pill?

▸ Show Answer

(a) Inner content width with box-sizing: border-box: The declared width: 160px includes border and padding. Content width = 160 − 2×2 (border) − 2×16 (padding) = 160 − 4 − 32 = 124px.

(b) border-radius: 50% would create a full ellipse (or circle if square), not a pill. On a 160×40px element, 50% would be 80px horizontal and 20px vertical radius, creating a distorted oval, not a pill. For a pill, you want the radius to equal half the shorter side (20px) at minimum.

(c) border-radius: 999px trick: Using a very large value ensures the radius is always clamped to half the element's height (20px here), creating a perfect pill regardless of the button's width. If you used 20px exactly and the button grew taller, it would no longer be fully rounded at the ends. The 999px is an "infinity hack" — it's capped by the browser to the maximum allowed, so it always produces fully rounded ends.

Question 48 — Multiple Choice

A <div> has the following CSS. What is its total rendered width (the space it takes up in the layout)?

div {
  box-sizing: content-box;
  width: 200px;
  padding: 20px;
  border: 5px solid black;
  margin: 10px;
}
  • A) 200px
  • B) 240px
  • C) 250px
  • D) 270px
▸ Show Answer

Answer: C) 250px

Calculation: With box-sizing: content-box (the default), width refers only to the content area.

  • Content: 200px
  • Left padding: 20px + Right padding: 20px = 40px
  • Left border: 5px + Right border: 5px = 10px
  • Rendered (border-box) width = 200 + 40 + 10 = 250px

Margin (10px each side = 20px total) is not included in the element's rendered width — it is space outside the element. Total space consumed in layout = 270px (250 + 20 margin), but the element's rendered width is 250px.

Bonus Questions — JavaScript, TypeScript & React

Question 1 — Multiple Choice

What does the following code print to the console?

const obj = {
  name: 'Alice',
  greet: () => {
    console.log(this.name);
  }
};
obj.greet();
  • A) 'Alice'
  • B) undefined
  • C) A ReferenceError
  • D) null
▸ Show Answer

B — undefined

Arrow functions do not have their own this. They capture this from the surrounding lexical scope at the time they are defined. Here the arrow function is defined at the top level (or inside a module), so this is the global object (or undefined in strict mode). Either way, this.name is not 'Alice'. This is the classic "method shorthand vs. arrow function" trap — arrow functions should not be used as object methods when you need this to refer to the object.

Question 2 — What Does This Print?

Predict the exact console output, in order:

function Counter() {
  this.count = 0;
  setInterval(function() {
    this.count++;
    console.log(this.count);
  }, 100);
}

const c = new Counter();
// (after 2 ticks)
▸ Show Answer

Output: NaN, NaN

The callback passed to setInterval is a regular function. When invoked by the timer, this is the global object (or undefined in strict mode), not the Counter instance. this.count on the global object is undefined, so undefined++ yields NaN.

Fix: Use an arrow function instead — setInterval(() => { this.count++; ... }, 100) — so that this is inherited from the constructor's lexical scope (the Counter instance).

Question 3 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following statements about arrow functions and this are correct?

  • [ ] A) Arrow functions have their own this determined at call time
  • [ ] B) Arrow functions capture this from their enclosing lexical scope at definition time
  • [ ] C) You can change the this of an arrow function using .call() or .apply()
  • [ ] D) Arrow functions are unsuitable as object method definitions when this needs to refer to the object
  • [ ] E) Arrow functions work well as callbacks inside regular methods because they inherit the method's this
  • [ ] F) Arrow functions can be used as constructors with new
▸ Show Answer

Correct: B, D, E

  • [x] B — Correct. this is lexically bound at definition time.
  • [ ] A — False. Arrow functions do NOT have their own this.
  • [ ] C — False. .call()/.apply() cannot override the lexically-bound this of an arrow function.
  • [x] D — Correct. As an object literal method, this would not refer to the object.
  • [x] E — Correct. This is the primary use-case: arrow function callbacks inside regular methods preserve the method's this.
  • [ ] F — False. Calling new on an arrow function throws a TypeError.

Question 4 — Fill in the Blanks

Complete the following sentences about the prototype chain:

Every JavaScript object has an internal link to another object called its 1. ________. When you access a property on an object and it is not found directly on that object, JavaScript walks up the 2. ________ until it finds it or reaches 3. ________, at which point it returns undefined. For objects created with a constructor function Foo, the prototype object is accessed via 4. ________. The instanceof operator checks whether 5. ________ appears anywhere in the prototype chain of an object.

▸ Show Answer

1. prototype (internal [[Prototype]], accessible via __proto__ or Object.getPrototypeOf())

2. prototype chain

3. null (the end of every prototype chain is Object.prototype, whose prototype is null)

4. Foo.prototype

5. Constructor.prototype (the constructor's prototype property)

Question 5 — Code Analysis

What is the output of the following code? Explain each line.

function Animal(name) {
  this.name = name;
}
Animal.prototype.speak = function() {
  return this.name + ' makes a noise.';
};

function Dog(name) {
  Animal.call(this, name);
}
Dog.prototype = Object.create(Animal.prototype);
Dog.prototype.constructor = Dog;

Dog.prototype.speak = function() {
  return this.name + ' barks.';
};

const d = new Dog('Rex');
console.log(d.speak());
console.log(d instanceof Dog);
console.log(d instanceof Animal);
console.log(Object.getPrototypeOf(d) === Dog.prototype);
▸ Show Answer

Output:

  1. 'Rex barks.' — Dog.prototype.speak overrides Animal.prototype.speak. Method lookup finds the override first.
  2. true — d was created with new Dog, so Dog.prototype is in its chain.
  3. true — Dog.prototype was set to Object.create(Animal.prototype), so Animal.prototype is also in the chain.
  4. true — Object.getPrototypeOf(d) returns Dog.prototype directly.

Key point: Dog.prototype.constructor = Dog is needed because Object.create(Animal.prototype) produces an object whose constructor property points to Animal. Without the reset, d.constructor === Animal would be true instead of Dog.

Question 6 — Multiple Choice

Which of the following correctly describes the three states of a JavaScript Promise?

  • A) loading, success, error
  • B) pending, fulfilled, rejected
  • C) waiting, resolved, failed
  • D) idle, complete, broken
▸ Show Answer

B — pending, fulfilled, rejected

A Promise starts as pending. It transitions to fulfilled when the async operation succeeds (the resolve callback is called) and to rejected when it fails (the reject callback is called). Once settled (fulfilled or rejected), a Promise cannot change state again. The collective term for fulfilled or rejected is settled.

Question 7 — What Does This Print?

Predict the exact console output, in order:

Promise.resolve(1)
  .then(v => { console.log(v); return v + 1; })
  .then(v => { throw new Error('oops'); })
  .then(v => { console.log('then:', v); })
  .catch(e => { console.log('caught:', e.message); return 99; })
  .then(v => { console.log('final:', v); });
▸ Show Answer

Output (in order):

  1. 1
  2. caught: oops
  3. final: 99

Explanation:

  • The first .then logs 1 and returns 2.
  • The second .then throws — the chain short-circuits, skipping the third .then.
  • .catch handles the error, logs 'caught: oops', and returns 99.
  • After .catch returns a non-throwing value, the chain resumes fulfilled with 99, so the final .then logs 'final: 99'.

Question 8 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following statements about Promise.allSettled vs Promise.all are correct?

  • [ ] A) Promise.all rejects immediately when any single promise rejects (short-circuit)
  • [ ] B) Promise.allSettled waits for every promise to settle before resolving, regardless of rejections
  • [ ] C) Promise.allSettled always resolves (never rejects) with an array of result descriptors
  • [ ] D) Each item in the Promise.allSettled result has a status field of either 'fulfilled' or 'rejected'
  • [ ] E) Promise.all resolves with the first fulfilled value, not an array
  • [ ] F) Promise.race resolves or rejects with whichever promise settles first
▸ Show Answer

Correct: A, B, C, D, F

  • [x] A — Promise.all short-circuits on the first rejection.
  • [x] B — Promise.allSettled waits for all.
  • [x] C — Promise.allSettled itself always resolves.
  • [x] D — Each descriptor is { status: 'fulfilled', value: ... } or { status: 'rejected', reason: ... }.
  • [ ] E — FALSE. Promise.all resolves with an array of all values (that's Promise.any for the first fulfilled).
  • [x] F — Promise.race settles (resolve or reject) with whichever promise settles first.

Question 9 — Fill in the Blanks

Complete the sentences about Promise combinators:

Promise.1.________ rejects as soon as any promise rejects, but resolves with an array of all values if all succeed. Promise.2.________ resolves as soon as any one promise fulfills, or rejects if all promises reject. Promise.3.________ settles (resolve or reject) with the outcome of whichever promise settles first. Promise.4.________ waits for every promise to settle and returns an array of status/value descriptors.

▸ Show Answer

1. all

2. any

3. race

4. allSettled

Question 10 — What Does This Print?

Predict the exact console output, in order. This is an event loop ordering puzzle.

console.log('A');

setTimeout(() => console.log('B'), 0);

Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('C'));

console.log('D');
▸ Show Answer

Output: A, D, C, B

Explanation:

  1. 'A' — synchronous, runs immediately.
  2. setTimeout callback is placed in the macrotask queue.
  3. The Promise.resolve().then callback is placed in the microtask queue.
  4. 'D' — synchronous, runs immediately.
  5. After the synchronous call stack is empty, the event loop drains the microtask queue first → 'C'.
  6. Then it picks the next macrotask → 'B'.

Rule: Microtasks (Promise callbacks, queueMicrotask) always run before the next macrotask (setTimeout, setInterval, I/O callbacks).

Question 11 — What Does This Print?

A more complex event loop puzzle. Predict the output in order:

console.log('start');

setTimeout(() => {
  console.log('timeout 1');
  Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('micro inside timeout'));
}, 0);

Promise.resolve()
  .then(() => {
    console.log('micro 1');
    return Promise.resolve();
  })
  .then(() => console.log('micro 2'));

setTimeout(() => console.log('timeout 2'), 0);

console.log('end');
▸ Show Answer

Output: start, end, micro 1, micro 2, timeout 1, micro inside timeout, timeout 2

Step-by-step:

  1. 'start' — sync
  2. 'end' — sync
  3. Microtask queue drains: 'micro 1'. Returning Promise.resolve() schedules another microtask.
  4. Microtask queue: 'micro 2'.
  5. First macrotask: 'timeout 1'. Its Promise.resolve().then is queued as a microtask.
  6. Microtask queue drains again before next macrotask: 'micro inside timeout'.
  7. Second macrotask: 'timeout 2'.

Key insight: After each macrotask, the entire microtask queue is drained before moving to the next macrotask.

Question 12 — Multiple Choice

Consider the following async/await code:

async function fetchUser(id) {
  const res = await fetch(`/api/users/${id}`);
  const data = await res.json();
  return data;
}

fetchUser(1).then(u => console.log(u.name));

The server responds with HTTP 404. Which of the following best describes what happens?

  • A) The fetch call throws a network error and fetchUser rejects
  • B) fetch resolves successfully; res.json() might succeed and data could be an error body; no automatic rejection occurs
  • C) res.json() throws because 404 responses have no body
  • D) async functions automatically throw when the response status is not 2xx
▸ Show Answer

B

The fetch API only rejects its Promise on a network failure (no connection, DNS error, etc.). A 404 is a valid HTTP response — fetch resolves with a Response object where res.ok === false and res.status === 404. The server may still send a JSON body (e.g., an error message). You must manually check res.ok and throw if needed:

if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP error: ${res.status}`);

Question 13 — Code Analysis (Written Short-Answer)

A developer writes the following to make a POST request with a JSON body. Identify ALL mistakes and provide the corrected version.

async function createPost(title, body) {
  const res = await fetch('/api/posts', {
    method: 'POST',
    body: { title, body }
  });
  return res.json();
}
▸ Show Answer

Two mistakes:

  1. Body is not serialized: body must be a string (or a Blob/FormData). Passing a plain object sends [object Object]. Fix: body: JSON.stringify({ title, body }).
  2. Missing Content-Type header: The server needs to know the body is JSON. Fix: add headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }.

Corrected code:

async function createPost(title, body) {
  const res = await fetch('/api/posts', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({ title, body })
  });
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${res.status}`);
  return res.json();
}

Question 14 — What Does This Print?

Predict the console output:

async function run() {
  console.log('1');
  const result = await Promise.resolve('2');
  console.log(result);
  console.log('3');
}

console.log('A');
run();
console.log('B');
▸ Show Answer

Output: A, 1, B, 2, 3

Explanation:

  1. 'A' — sync before run() is called.
  2. '1' — the body of run executes synchronously up to the first await.
  3. At await, execution suspends and control returns to the caller.
  4. 'B' — sync code after run() continues.
  5. The call stack is now empty; microtask queue runs: '2', then '3'.

Key: await always yields execution back to the caller, even when awaiting an already-resolved Promise. The continuation is scheduled as a microtask.

Question 15 — Written Short-Answer

Explain the difference between interface and type in TypeScript. Give one thing each can do that the other cannot.

▸ Show Answer

Similarities: Both can describe the shape of objects, be used as type annotations, and be extended/intersected.

What interface can do that type cannot:

  • Declaration merging — declaring the same interface name twice causes TypeScript to merge them. This is how library authors augment existing types.
interface User { name: string; }
interface User { age: number; }
// merged: { name: string; age: number }

What type can do that interface cannot:

  • Union and intersection types, mapped types, conditional types, tuples as top-level aliases.
type StringOrNumber = string | number;
type Nullable<T> = T | null;

Common advice: Use interface for object shapes (especially in public APIs), use type for unions, primitives, and complex type expressions.

Question 16 — Multiple Choice

What is the difference between any and unknown in TypeScript?

  • A) They are identical — both opt out of type checking entirely
  • B) any allows all operations without type checking; unknown requires a type check (narrowing) before you can perform operations on the value
  • C) unknown is only for function return types; any is for parameters
  • D) any causes a compile error; unknown is the safe alternative
▸ Show Answer

B

any is a full escape hatch — you can call methods, access properties, or pass it anywhere. unknown is the type-safe counterpart: you must first narrow the type (with typeof, instanceof, or a type guard) before performing any operations. Use unknown when you genuinely don't know a type but still want the compiler to enforce safety.

let x: unknown = getData();
x.toUpperCase();           // TS ERROR
if (typeof x === 'string') x.toUpperCase(); // OK

Question 17 — Fill in the Blanks

Complete the following about TypeScript utility types:

1.________<T> makes all properties of T optional. 2.________<T> makes all properties required. 3.________<T> makes all properties read-only. 4.________<T, K> picks only the specified keys K from type T. 5.________<T, K> creates a type with all keys from T except those in K. 6.________<K, V> constructs a type with keys from K and values of type V.

▸ Show Answer

1. Partial

2. Required

3. Readonly

4. Pick

5. Omit

6. Record

Question 18 — Code Analysis

What TypeScript errors (if any) exist in the following code? Explain each one.

function identity<T>(value: T): T {
  return value;
}

const result1 = identity<string>(42);
const result2 = identity('hello');
const result3: number = identity<number>(100);
▸ Show Answer

Only one error: result1

identity<string>(42) — the type parameter is explicitly set to string, but the argument 42 is a number. TypeScript will error: Argument of type 'number' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'.

result2 is fine — TypeScript infers T = string from the argument 'hello'.

result3 is fine — explicit generic with matching argument type, and the return type is annotated correctly.

Question 19 — Multiple Choice

What does keyof return in TypeScript?

interface Car {
  make: string;
  model: string;
  year: number;
}

type CarKey = keyof Car;
  • A) string
  • B) { make: string; model: string; year: number }
  • C) 'make' | 'model' | 'year'
  • D) string[]
▸ Show Answer

C — 'make' | 'model' | 'year'

keyof T produces a union of the string literal types of all keys of T. It is commonly used with generics to create type-safe property accessor functions:

function getProperty<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K] {
  return obj[key];
}

Question 20 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following are valid TypeScript type narrowing techniques?

  • [ ] A) typeof x === 'string'
  • [ ] B) x instanceof Date
  • [ ] C) 'swim' in animal (checking if a property exists)
  • [ ] D) A user-defined type guard function returning x is SomeType
  • [ ] E) Assigning a value to a variable annotated with a narrower type
  • [ ] F) Using the as keyword to cast a value
▸ Show Answer

Correct: A, B, C, D

  • [x] A — typeof guard narrows primitive types.
  • [x] B — instanceof narrows class instances.
  • [x] C — in operator checks for property existence and narrows union members.
  • [x] D — User-defined type guards (predicate: x is T) are a core TypeScript narrowing mechanism.
  • [ ] E — FALSE. Assigning to a narrower annotation is a compile error, not narrowing.
  • [ ] F — FALSE. as is a type assertion (you override the compiler), not narrowing. It can hide bugs.

Question 21 — Written Short-Answer

Explain the difference between never and void in TypeScript. When would you use each?

▸ Show Answer

void means a function returns no meaningful value (it returns undefined). It is used for callbacks and functions whose return value is irrelevant.

never means a function never returns at all — it either throws unconditionally, or runs an infinite loop. It is also the type inferred for unreachable code and for the bottom of exhaustive type checks.

// void — returns, but the return value is unused
function log(msg: string): void {
  console.log(msg);
}

// never — throws unconditionally, never returns
function fail(msg: string): never {
  throw new Error(msg);
}

// never — exhaustiveness check
function assertNever(x: never): never {
  throw new Error('Unhandled case: ' + x);
}

Key distinction: A void function reaches its end; a never function cannot.

Question 22 — Multiple Choice

What is the type inferred for status in the following TypeScript code?

const status = 'loading' as const;
type StatusType = typeof status;
  • A) string
  • B) 'loading' (string literal type)
  • C) const
  • D) string | undefined
▸ Show Answer

B — 'loading'

Without as const, TypeScript widens the type to string. With as const, the value is treated as a literal type — the narrowest possible type. This is essential when building discriminated unions, keyof operations on constant objects, and exhaustive checks.

Question 23 — Code Analysis

Identify the Rules of Hooks violation(s) in the component below and explain why each is a problem:

function UserList({ isAdmin }) {
  const [users, setUsers] = useState([]);

  if (isAdmin) {
    const [adminData, setAdminData] = useState(null);
  }

  for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    useEffect(() => {
      console.log('effect', i);
    }, []);
  }

  return <div>{users.length}</div>;
}
▸ Show Answer

Two violations:

  1. Hook inside a conditional (if (isAdmin)): useState is called only when isAdmin is true. React relies on the order of hook calls being identical on every render. If isAdmin changes between renders, the hook call order changes, causing React to mismap hook state to the wrong slot — leading to corrupted state or crashes.
  2. Hook inside a loop (for loop): Same principle — useEffect is called a variable number of times. Hook calls must be unconditional and at the top level of the component function.

Fix: Move all hooks to the top level. Use conditional logic inside the hook's callback, not around the hook call itself.

Question 24 — What Does This Print? (React)

What value is alerted when the button is clicked, and why?

function Counter() {
  const [count, setCount] = useState(0);

  const handleClick = () => {
    setTimeout(() => {
      alert(count);
    }, 3000);
    setCount(count + 1);
  };

  return <button onClick={handleClick}>Click me ({count})</button>;
}

The user clicks the button once. Three seconds later, what does the alert show?

▸ Show Answer

The alert shows 0 (the count value at the time the click handler ran).

This is a stale closure. The handleClick arrow function closes over the count variable from the render it was created in. At the time of the click, count is 0. Even though setCount(1) triggers a re-render, the setTimeout callback still holds a reference to the old count = 0 closure, not the new render's count = 1.

Fix: Use a ref to always read the latest value:

const countRef = useRef(count);
useEffect(() => { countRef.current = count; }, [count]);
// then inside setTimeout: alert(countRef.current);

Question 25 — Multiple Choice

A component receives an <input> with both a value prop and an onChange handler. What kind of component is this?

  • A) Uncontrolled — the DOM owns the input's state
  • B) Controlled — React state owns the input's value
  • C) Hybrid — both React and the DOM share ownership
  • D) Ref-controlled — only a ref manages the input
▸ Show Answer

B — Controlled

A controlled component has its form data driven by React state. The value prop locks the input to the state value; the onChange handler updates state in response to user input. This gives React full control over the input's value at all times.

An uncontrolled component uses defaultValue (or no value prop at all) and relies on a ref to read the DOM value imperatively when needed.

Question 26 — Code Analysis

Explain the bug in the following useState usage and provide the correct fix:

function TodoApp() {
  const [todos, setTodos] = useState([]);

  const addTodo = (text) => {
    todos.push({ id: Date.now(), text });
    setTodos(todos);
  };

  return (
    <div>
      {todos.map(t => <p key={t.id}>{t.text}</p>)}
      <button onClick={() => addTodo('new item')}>Add</button>
    </div>
  );
}
▸ Show Answer

Bug: mutating state directly. todos.push(...) mutates the existing array in place. Then setTodos(todos) passes the same array reference. React uses Object.is (reference equality) to decide whether to re-render. Since the reference hasn't changed, React may skip the re-render entirely.

Correct fix — always produce a new array:

const addTodo = (text) => {
  setTodos(prev => [...prev, { id: Date.now(), text }]);
};

Using the functional updater form (prev => ...) is also safer if multiple state updates are batched, as it always operates on the latest state.

Question 27 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following are valid useEffect dependency array patterns and their corresponding behaviors?

  • [ ] A) No second argument — effect runs after every render
  • [ ] B) Empty array [] — effect runs once on mount; cleanup runs on unmount
  • [ ] C) [id] — effect runs on mount and whenever id changes
  • [ ] D) [id] — effect runs only when id first becomes truthy
  • [ ] E) An effect with a return function — the returned function runs before the next effect and on unmount
  • [ ] F) Putting a function created inside the component in deps without wrapping in useCallback can cause the effect to re-run every render
▸ Show Answer

Correct: A, B, C, E, F

  • [x] A — No deps array → runs after every render.
  • [x] B — [] → runs once on mount.
  • [x] C — [id] → runs on mount and on every change of id.
  • [ ] D — FALSE. React doesn't care whether id is truthy; it runs whenever id's value changes from the previous render.
  • [x] E — The cleanup function runs before the next effect execution and on unmount.
  • [x] F — A new function object is created every render, so the dependency changes every render, triggering the effect repeatedly.

Question 28 — Multiple Choice

What is the primary purpose of the key prop in React lists?

  • A) To give each list item a CSS class for styling
  • B) To help React's reconciliation algorithm identify which items have changed, been added, or removed
  • C) To ensure items are rendered in alphabetical order
  • D) To pass a unique identifier to the item's child components
▸ Show Answer

B

The key prop is a hint to the React reconciler. When a list re-renders, React uses keys to match old virtual DOM nodes to new ones. Without stable unique keys, React falls back to index-based matching, which can cause incorrect re-use of component instances when items are reordered, inserted, or deleted — leading to subtle state bugs.

Do not use array index as key when the list can be reordered or items can be inserted/removed. Use a stable, unique ID instead.

Question 29 — Code Analysis

Explain the difference between the two useReducer approaches below and when you'd prefer one over the other:

// Approach A
const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
const increment = () => setCount(count + 1);
const decrement = () => setCount(count - 1);
const reset = () => setCount(0);

// Approach B
const initialState = { count: 0 };
function reducer(state, action) {
  switch (action.type) {
    case 'increment': return { count: state.count + 1 };
    case 'decrement': return { count: state.count - 1 };
    case 'reset':     return initialState;
    default: throw new Error('Unknown action: ' + action.type);
  }
}
const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, initialState);
▸ Show Answer

Approach A (useState) is simpler and appropriate for independent, simple state updates.

Approach B (useReducer) is better when:

  • State updates involve multiple sub-values (e.g., a form with many fields).
  • The next state depends on the previous in complex ways.
  • You want the update logic in one testable place (the reducer), separate from the component.
  • You pass dispatch deep via context, avoiding the need to pass many setter functions.

Both are valid; useReducer scales better with complex state machines.

Question 30 — Fill in the Blanks

Complete the following about useRef:

useRef returns a mutable object with a single property: 1. ________. Changes to this property do not cause a 2. ________. This makes it suitable for storing values that need to persist across renders without triggering UI updates, such as 3. ________ IDs, previous state values, or DOM 4. ________. To attach a ref to a DOM element, pass it as the element's 5. ________ prop.

▸ Show Answer

1. current

2. re-render

3. timer / interval (e.g., from setTimeout or setInterval)

4. element references (DOM nodes)

5. ref

Question 31 — Code Analysis

What is the output of the following React-like pseudocode? Describe the render and effect sequence.

function App() {
  const [count, setCount] = useState(0);

  console.log('render', count);

  useEffect(() => {
    console.log('effect', count);
    return () => console.log('cleanup', count);
  }, [count]);

  return <button onClick={() => setCount(c => c + 1)}>{count}</button>;
}

User clicks the button twice. List all console outputs in order.

▸ Show Answer

Output in order:

  1. render 0 — initial render
  2. effect 0 — effect runs after initial render
  3. render 1 — after first click
  4. cleanup 0 — cleanup from previous effect (with old count = 0)
  5. effect 1 — new effect with count = 1
  6. render 2 — after second click
  7. cleanup 1 — cleanup with count = 1
  8. effect 2 — new effect with count = 2

Key: The cleanup function captures the old value of count from its closure and runs before the new effect fires. Effects run after the browser has painted.

Question 32 — Multiple Choice

When should you use React.memo?

  • A) Always — it makes every component faster
  • B) On components that receive the same props frequently and are expensive to re-render, to skip unnecessary re-renders
  • C) Only on class components
  • D) On components that manage their own state and never receive props
▸ Show Answer

B

React.memo wraps a component and performs a shallow prop comparison before re-rendering. If the props haven't changed, the previous render result is reused. It is useful when:

  • The component renders the same output for the same props.
  • The component is re-rendered often by a parent that changes other state.
  • The component is expensive to render (large lists, complex computations).

Not a silver bullet: It adds the overhead of prop comparison on every render. If props change nearly every render, React.memo adds cost without benefit. Always profile first.

Question 33 — Code Analysis

The following component uses React.memo and useCallback but still re-renders the child on every parent render. Identify why and fix it.

const Child = React.memo(({ onClick }) => {
  console.log('Child rendered');
  return <button onClick={onClick}>Click</button>;
});

function Parent() {
  const [count, setCount] = useState(0);

  const handleClick = () => {
    setCount(c => c + 1);
  };

  return (
    <div>
      <p>{count}</p>
      <Child onClick={handleClick} />
    </div>
  );
}
▸ Show Answer

Problem: handleClick is a regular function created fresh on every render of Parent. Even though Child is wrapped in React.memo, the shallow prop comparison sees a new function reference for onClick every time — so it re-renders.

Fix: Wrap handleClick in useCallback with a stable dependency array:

const handleClick = useCallback(() => {
  setCount(c => c + 1);
}, []); // empty deps — uses functional updater so no closure over count

Now handleClick is the same reference across renders, and React.memo's shallow comparison will correctly skip re-rendering Child.

Question 34 — Written Short-Answer

Explain the concept of "lifting state up" in React. Give a concrete scenario where you would apply it.

▸ Show Answer

Lifting state up means moving shared state to the closest common ancestor of the components that need to read or update it. React data flows one-way (parent to child), so the only way two sibling components can share state is if their parent owns it and passes it down as props (with callback props for updates).

Scenario: You have a temperature converter with a Celsius input and a Fahrenheit input. Each needs to reflect the value the other changes. Solution: lift the temperature value (and its setter) up to the parent. Both inputs receive the current value as a prop and call a parent-provided handler on change.

function TemperatureConverter() {
  const [celsius, setCelsius] = useState(0);
  return (
    <>
      <CelsiusInput value={celsius} onChange={setCelsius} />
      <FahrenheitInput
        value={celsius * 9/5 + 32}
        onChange={f => setCelsius((f - 32) * 5/9)}
      />
    </>
  );
}

Question 35 — Code Analysis

What does the following code do? Explain forwardRef and useImperativeHandle.

const FancyInput = React.forwardRef((props, ref) => {
  const inputRef = useRef(null);

  useImperativeHandle(ref, () => ({
    focus: () => inputRef.current.focus(),
    clear: () => { inputRef.current.value = ''; }
  }));

  return <input ref={inputRef} {...props} />;
});

function Form() {
  const fancyRef = useRef(null);
  return (
    <>
      <FancyInput ref={fancyRef} />
      <button onClick={() => fancyRef.current.focus()}>Focus</button>
      <button onClick={() => fancyRef.current.clear()}>Clear</button>
    </>
  );
}
▸ Show Answer

forwardRef allows a parent component to pass a ref down to a child component's DOM element (or to a custom imperative handle). Without it, refs on function components are ignored.

useImperativeHandle customizes what value is exposed to the parent when they hold a ref to the child. Instead of exposing the raw DOM node (which would let the parent do anything), the child exposes only a controlled API — in this case focus() and clear() methods.

Effect: fancyRef.current in Form is the object { focus: fn, clear: fn }, not the raw <input> DOM element. Clicking "Focus" calls the exposed focus method, and clicking "Clear" zeroes the input's value.

When to use: Use useImperativeHandle sparingly — for exposing a minimal imperative API (focus management, animation triggers, scroll commands) while keeping the internal implementation encapsulated.

Question 36 — Fill in the Blanks

Complete the following about useContext:

To share data without prop drilling, first create a context with 1. ________. Wrap the tree that needs access in a 2. ________ component and pass the shared value via its 3. ________ prop. Any descendant component can read the value by calling the 4. ________ hook with the context object. The component re-renders whenever the context 5. ________ changes. To avoid unnecessary re-renders, combine context with 6. ________ to stabilize values.

▸ Show Answer

1. React.createContext(defaultValue)

2. Context.Provider

3. value

4. useContext

5. value

6. useMemo (or useCallback for function values) — so that the context value reference only changes when its contents actually change, not on every parent render.

Question 37 — Multiple Choice

What is the main difference between useMemo and useCallback?

  • A) useMemo runs effects; useCallback memoizes components
  • B) useMemo memoizes a computed value; useCallback memoizes a function reference
  • C) They are identical — useCallback(fn, deps) is syntactic sugar for useMemo(() => fn, deps)
  • D) useMemo only works with arrays; useCallback works with any type
▸ Show Answer

B — and actually C is also technically true. In React's implementation, useCallback(fn, deps) is equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, deps). Both memoize a value across renders. The distinction is semantic: useMemo is idiomatic for expensive computed values (filtered arrays, derived objects), and useCallback is idiomatic for stable function references passed as props. The "best" answer here is B — it describes the intended use case distinction.

Question 38 — Written Short-Answer

What is a custom hook in React? Write a simple custom hook called useWindowWidth that tracks the browser window's width and returns it.

▸ Show Answer

A custom hook is a regular JavaScript function whose name starts with use that can call other hooks. Custom hooks let you extract and reuse stateful logic across multiple components without duplicating code or changing the component tree.

function useWindowWidth() {
  const [width, setWidth] = useState(window.innerWidth);

  useEffect(() => {
    const handleResize = () => setWidth(window.innerWidth);
    window.addEventListener('resize', handleResize);
    return () => window.removeEventListener('resize', handleResize);
  }, []); // empty deps — listener registered once, cleaned up on unmount

  return width;
}

// Usage
function MyComponent() {
  const width = useWindowWidth();
  return <p>Window is {width}px wide</p>;
}

Rules: Custom hooks must follow the Rules of Hooks — only call hooks at the top level, never inside conditions or loops.

Question 39 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following violate the Rules of Hooks?

// A
function useData(id) {
  if (id) {
    const [data, setData] = useState(null);
  }
}

// B
function MyComponent() {
  const handlers = ['click', 'hover'].map(type =>
    useCallback(() => console.log(type), [type])
  );
}

// C
function useTimer() {
  const [tick, setTick] = useState(0);
  useEffect(() => {
    const id = setInterval(() => setTick(t => t + 1), 1000);
    return () => clearInterval(id);
  }, []);
  return tick;
}

// D
class Counter extends React.Component {
  render() {
    const [n, setN] = useState(0); // inside class component
    return <div>{n}</div>;
  }
}
▸ Show Answer

Violations: A, B, D

  • [x] A — Hook inside a conditional block. Breaks the ordering guarantee.
  • [x] B — Hook inside a map loop. Hooks must not be called inside loops.
  • [ ] C — Valid. useTimer is a properly written custom hook: hooks at the top level, no conditionals.
  • [x] D — Hooks cannot be used inside class components. They are only valid in function components and custom hooks.

Question 40 — What Does This Print?

Predict the complete console output of the following async/await code:

async function fetchA() {
  console.log('fetchA start');
  await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  console.log('fetchA end');
  return 'A';
}

async function fetchB() {
  console.log('fetchB start');
  const result = await fetchA();
  console.log('fetchB got:', result);
  return 'B';
}

console.log('main start');
fetchB();
console.log('main end');
▸ Show Answer

Output: main start, fetchB start, fetchA start, main end, fetchA end, fetchB got: A

Step-by-step:

  1. 'main start' — sync
  2. fetchB() starts; 'fetchB start' — sync inside async function up to first await
  3. fetchA() starts; 'fetchA start' — sync inside fetchA up to its await
  4. At await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 0)), a macrotask is scheduled; fetchA suspends, fetchB suspends, control returns to the caller.
  5. 'main end' — sync code after fetchB() continues
  6. Macrotask fires, resolving the Promise inside fetchA; fetchA resumes as a microtask: 'fetchA end'
  7. fetchA returns 'A'; fetchB resumes: 'fetchB got: A'

Question 41 — Multiple Choice

In TypeScript, what is the difference between a const enum and a regular enum?

  • A) They are identical at runtime
  • B) const enum members are inlined at compile time and leave no runtime object; regular enum compiles to a real JavaScript object
  • C) const enum allows string values; regular enum only allows numbers
  • D) Regular enum supports reverse mapping for numbers; const enum adds that plus string reverse mapping
▸ Show Answer

B

A regular enum compiles to a JavaScript object that exists at runtime:

// TypeScript enum
enum Direction { Up = 1, Down }
// Compiled JS
var Direction;
(function (Direction) {
  Direction[Direction["Up"] = 1] = "Up";
  Direction[Direction["Down"] = 2] = "Down";
})(Direction || (Direction = {}));

A const enum is erased entirely — each reference is replaced with its literal value inline. This produces smaller output but means you cannot iterate over the enum at runtime or use it with Object.keys().

Question 42 — Code Analysis

Explain what this TypeScript generic utility type does and give an example of its use:

type ReturnType<T extends (...args: any) => any> =
  T extends (...args: any) => infer R ? R : never;
▸ Show Answer

This is a conditional type using the infer keyword. It extracts the return type of a function type T.

  • T extends (...args: any) => infer R — if T is a function type, TypeScript "infers" (captures) the return type into the type variable R.
  • If it matches, the result is R (the return type); otherwise never.

Example:

function greet(name: string): string { return `Hello, ${name}`; }
type GreetReturn = ReturnType<typeof greet>; // string

async function fetchUser(): Promise<User> { ... }
type FetchReturn = ReturnType<typeof fetchUser>; // Promise<User>

This is actually the built-in ReturnType<T> utility type — used to derive types from existing functions without repeating yourself.

Question 43 — Multiple Choice

What is the difference between a TypeScript tuple and a regular array type?

  • A) Tuples are for objects; regular arrays are for primitives
  • B) A tuple has a fixed length and each position has a specific type; a regular array has homogeneous elements of one type
  • C) Tuples can only contain strings; arrays can contain any type
  • D) Tuples are mutable; regular arrays are read-only in TypeScript
▸ Show Answer

B

// Regular array: all elements are the same type
const names: string[] = ['Alice', 'Bob'];

// Tuple: fixed length, specific type per position
const point: [number, number] = [10, 20];
const entry: [string, number] = ['age', 25];

// Useful for function return values with mixed types
function useState<T>(init: T): [T, (val: T) => void] { ... }

TypeScript uses tuples heavily for hook return types and when you want to destructure a fixed-structure return value rather than a named object.

Question 44 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following are true about the virtual DOM and React's reconciliation process?

  • [ ] A) React always updates the entire real DOM when any state changes
  • [ ] B) React creates a virtual DOM tree — a JavaScript object representation of the UI
  • [ ] C) On a state change, React diffs the new virtual DOM against the previous one (reconciliation)
  • [ ] D) React updates only the real DOM nodes that correspond to changed virtual DOM nodes
  • [ ] E) Changing an element's type (e.g., from <div> to <span>) causes React to reuse the existing DOM node
  • [ ] F) Stable key props help React correctly identify which list items changed during reconciliation
▸ Show Answer

Correct: B, C, D, F

  • [ ] A — FALSE. React only updates changed parts.
  • [x] B — Correct. The virtual DOM is a lightweight JS representation.
  • [x] C — Correct. Diffing is the reconciliation algorithm.
  • [x] D — Correct. Only the minimum set of real DOM updates is applied.
  • [ ] E — FALSE. When an element's type changes, React destroys the existing tree and mounts a new one from scratch (it does not reuse the DOM node).
  • [x] F — Correct. Stable keys allow React to match old nodes to new ones accurately.

Question 45 — Written Short-Answer

Describe what happens in each of the four this-binding rules in JavaScript. For each rule, give a one-line code example showing when it applies.

▸ Show Answer

1. Default binding — applies when a function is called as a plain function (no object, no new, no explicit binding). this is the global object (or undefined in strict mode).

function greet() { console.log(this); }
greet(); // window (or undefined in strict)

2. Implicit binding — applies when a function is called as a method on an object. this is the object to the left of the dot.

const obj = { name: 'Bob', greet() { console.log(this.name); } };
obj.greet(); // 'Bob'

3. Explicit binding — applies when .call(), .apply(), or .bind() is used to explicitly set this.

greet.call({ name: 'Carol' }); // 'Carol'

4. new binding — applies when a function is called with the new keyword. A new empty object is created, this is set to it, and the function populates it.

function Person(name) { this.name = name; }
const p = new Person('Dave'); // this === p inside the function

Priority: new > explicit (.call/.bind) > implicit (method) > default.

Question 46 — Code Analysis

The following TypeScript code has a type error. Identify it and explain how to fix it using a type guard:

function processInput(input: string | number) {
  console.log(input.toUpperCase());
}
▸ Show Answer

Error: toUpperCase() is a method on string, but input could be a number. TypeScript will raise: Property 'toUpperCase' does not exist on type 'string | number'.

Fix using a typeof type guard:

function processInput(input: string | number) {
  if (typeof input === 'string') {
    console.log(input.toUpperCase()); // narrowed to string here
  } else {
    console.log(input.toFixed(2));    // narrowed to number here
  }
}

Inside each branch, TypeScript narrows the type based on the typeof check, allowing safe access to type-specific methods.

Question 47 — Fill in the Blanks

Complete the following about class syntax in JavaScript and TypeScript:

A class in JavaScript is syntactic sugar over 1. ________ -based inheritance. The special method 2. ________ is called when a class is instantiated with new. To inherit from another class, use the 3. ________ keyword. In the subclass constructor, you must call 4. ________ before accessing this. In TypeScript, class members can be marked as 5. ________ (accessible only within the class), 6. ________ (accessible within the class and subclasses), or default to public.

▸ Show Answer

1. prototype

2. constructor

3. extends

4. super()

5. private

6. protected

Question 48 — What Does This Print?

Predict all console outputs in order:

class Base {
  constructor() {
    console.log('Base constructor');
    this.init();
  }
  init() {
    console.log('Base init');
  }
}

class Child extends Base {
  constructor() {
    super();
    console.log('Child constructor');
  }
  init() {
    console.log('Child init');
  }
}

const c = new Child();
▸ Show Answer

Output: Base constructor, Child init, Child constructor

Explanation:

  1. new Child() calls Child's constructor, which immediately calls super().
  2. super() runs the Base constructor: 'Base constructor'.
  3. Inside Base's constructor, this.init() is called. Because this is the Child instance (JavaScript uses dynamic dispatch), Child.prototype.init is called: 'Child init'.
  4. Back in Child's constructor after super() returns: 'Child constructor'.

This demonstrates polymorphic dispatch — the method called depends on the runtime type of this, not the class whose constructor is running.

Question 49 — Multiple Choice

A useEffect hook fetches data and sets state. The component unmounts before the fetch completes. What is the best way to prevent the "Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component" warning?

  • A) Wrap setData in a try/catch
  • B) Use useRef to track if the component is still mounted, and check before calling setState; or use the cleanup function to cancel the fetch using AbortController
  • C) Add the fetch URL to the effect's dependency array
  • D) Switch to a class component, which handles unmounting automatically
▸ Show Answer

B

The modern best practice is to use AbortController to cancel the in-flight request in the effect's cleanup function:

useEffect(() => {
  const controller = new AbortController();
  fetch('/api/data', { signal: controller.signal })
    .then(res => res.json())
    .then(data => setData(data))
    .catch(err => {
      if (err.name !== 'AbortError') console.error(err);
    });
  return () => controller.abort(); // cleanup: cancel the request
}, []);

The isMounted ref pattern also works but AbortController is preferred because it actually cancels the network request, saving bandwidth.

Question 50 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following statements about the JavaScript event loop are correct?

  • [ ] A) The call stack is single-threaded — only one piece of code executes at a time
  • [ ] B) setTimeout(fn, 0) guarantees fn will run after exactly 0 milliseconds
  • [ ] C) Microtasks (Promise callbacks) are processed between each macrotask
  • [ ] D) Multiple microtasks can be queued and all of them run before the next macrotask is picked up
  • [ ] E) requestAnimationFrame callbacks run as microtasks
  • [ ] F) MutationObserver callbacks are microtasks
▸ Show Answer

Correct: A, C, D, F

  • [x] A — The JS call stack is single-threaded.
  • [ ] B — FALSE. setTimeout(fn, 0) schedules fn at the earliest opportunity after the current task and all microtasks finish — browser minimum is typically 4ms+ when nested; it is never guaranteed to be exact.
  • [x] C — Microtasks run after each macrotask and after each task's synchronous code completes.
  • [x] D — The entire microtask queue is drained before the next macrotask.
  • [ ] E — FALSE. requestAnimationFrame is a macrotask (it runs before paint, but it's not a microtask).
  • [x] F — MutationObserver callbacks are dispatched as microtasks.

Question 51 — Code Analysis

The following component has a performance problem. Identify it and propose a fix.

function ExpensiveList({ items }) {
  const sorted = items
    .filter(i => i.active)
    .sort((a, b) => a.name.localeCompare(b.name));

  return (
    <ul>
      {sorted.map(i => <li key={i.id}>{i.name}</li>)}
    </ul>
  );
}

The parent component re-renders frequently for unrelated state changes. items itself rarely changes. What is the problem and how do you fix it?

▸ Show Answer

Problem: The filter + sort computation runs on every render of ExpensiveList, even when items hasn't changed. If items is a large array, this is wasteful.

Fix — two complementary steps:

  1. Wrap the component in React.memo to skip re-renders when items reference hasn't changed.
  2. Memoize the expensive computation with useMemo so it only recalculates when items changes:
const ExpensiveList = React.memo(function ExpensiveList({ items }) {
  const sorted = useMemo(() =>
    items
      .filter(i => i.active)
      .sort((a, b) => a.name.localeCompare(b.name)),
    [items]
  );

  return (
    <ul>
      {sorted.map(i => <li key={i.id}>{i.name}</li>)}
    </ul>
  );
});

Question 52 — Written Short-Answer

Explain the difference between Promise.race and Promise.any. Give a practical use case for each.

▸ Show Answer

Promise.race(promises) — settles with whatever promise settles first, whether fulfilled or rejected. If the first promise to settle is rejected, race rejects too.

Use case: Implementing a timeout for a network request:

const result = await Promise.race([
  fetch('/api/data'),
  new Promise((_, reject) =>
    setTimeout(() => reject(new Error('Timeout')), 5000))
]);

Promise.any(promises) — resolves as soon as any one promise fulfills, ignoring rejections. Only rejects (with an AggregateError) if all promises reject.

Use case: Fetching from multiple CDN mirrors and using whichever responds first:

const asset = await Promise.any([
  fetch('https://cdn1.example.com/asset.js'),
  fetch('https://cdn2.example.com/asset.js'),
  fetch('https://cdn3.example.com/asset.js'),
]);

Key distinction: race cares about the first settlement (reject or resolve); any cares only about the first fulfillment.

Bonus Questions — Next.js, State & Forms

SECTION A: Next.js App Router — Routing & Special Files

Question 1 — Multiple Choice

In the Next.js App Router, which file defines the UI that is shared across a route segment and its children, and persists across navigations without re-mounting?

  • A) template.tsx
  • B) page.tsx
  • C) layout.tsx
  • D) default.tsx
▸ Show Answer

Answer: C

Explanation: layout.tsx wraps a segment and all its children. Unlike template.tsx, a layout preserves its state and does not re-mount on navigation. template.tsx is the same shape but creates a new instance on every navigation — useful when you need fresh state or lifecycle effects per visit.

Question 2 — Fill in the Blanks

Match each App Router special file to its purpose:

1. ________ — shown while a route segment and its children are loading (enables streaming).
2. ________ — shown when an unhandled error is thrown inside a segment (must be a Client Component).
3. ________ — shown when notFound() is called or a segment has no match.
4. ________ — handles HTTP methods (GET, POST, etc.) for an API endpoint inside the app/ directory.
5. ________ — re-creates its subtree on every navigation, unlike a layout.

▸ Show Answer

1. loading.tsx

2. error.tsx

3. not-found.tsx

4. route.ts

5. template.tsx

Question 3 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following are valid dynamic segment conventions in the Next.js App Router?

  • [ ] [slug] — matches a single dynamic segment
  • [ ] [...slug] — catch-all, matches one or more segments
  • [ ] [[...slug]] — optional catch-all, matches zero or more segments
  • [ ] {slug} — curly-brace syntax for dynamic segments
  • [ ] (*slug) — wildcard syntax
  • [ ] (slug) — route group, does NOT create a URL segment
▸ Show Answer

Correct answers:

  • [x] [slug] — single dynamic segment
  • [x] [...slug] — catch-all (one or more)
  • [x] [[...slug]] — optional catch-all (zero or more; the route also matches with no segment)
  • [ ] {slug} — FALSE, not a valid convention
  • [ ] (*slug) — FALSE, not a valid convention
  • [x] (slug) — route group; parentheses exclude the folder name from the URL but still organize the file system. Note: this is a route group, not a dynamic segment.

Question 4 — Written Answer

Explain the difference between a parallel route and an intercepting route in Next.js. Give a concrete use-case for each.

▸ Show Answer

Parallel routes (@slot folders) allow multiple pages to be rendered simultaneously in the same layout. They are defined with the @folderName convention and are passed as props to the parent layout. Example use-case: a dashboard with @analytics and @team panels side-by-side that can load and navigate independently.

Intercepting routes ((.)folder, (..)folder, etc.) let you render a route in the context of the current layout, intercepting navigation. Classic use-case: clicking a photo in a feed shows it in a modal (intercepted route) while a direct URL or page-refresh shows it on its own dedicated page (the real route).

Folder prefixes: (.) same level, (..) one level up, (...) from root.

Question 5 — Multiple Choice

You create the file app/(marketing)/about/page.tsx. What URL does this page respond to?

  • A) /marketing/about
  • B) /(marketing)/about
  • C) /about
  • D) /marketing
▸ Show Answer

Answer: C

Explanation: Parenthesised folders are route groups — they organise files but are excluded from the URL path. Therefore (marketing) is invisible in the URL and the route resolves to /about.

Question 6 — Multiple Choice

Which file inside app/ is used to define a Route Handler (API endpoint) that responds to HTTP requests?

  • A) api.ts
  • B) handler.ts
  • C) route.ts
  • D) endpoint.ts
▸ Show Answer

Answer: C

Explanation: Route Handlers live in route.ts (or route.js) files. They export named functions matching HTTP verbs: export async function GET(request: Request) {}, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc. A route.ts and a page.tsx cannot coexist in the same folder.

SECTION B: Server vs Client Components

Question 7 — Multiple Choice

Which of the following is the correct reason to add "use client" at the top of a file in Next.js App Router?

  • A) To enable SSR for the component so it renders on the server
  • B) To mark the component and all its imported subtree as client-side, allowing React hooks and browser APIs
  • C) To prevent the component from being included in the server bundle
  • D) To opt out of automatic static generation for that page
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: "use client" declares a client boundary. The directive applies to the file and every module it imports. This makes React hooks (useState, useEffect, etc.) and browser-only APIs available. Without this directive, components are Server Components by default in the App Router.

Question 8 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following can a Server Component do that a Client Component cannot?

  • [ ] Access a database or filesystem directly without an API layer
  • [ ] Use useState and useEffect
  • [ ] Keep sensitive credentials (API keys) out of the client bundle
  • [ ] Use event handlers like onClick
  • [ ] Fetch data with async/await at the component level
  • [ ] Use the browser localStorage API
▸ Show Answer

Correct (Server Component can do these):

  • [x] Access a database or filesystem directly
  • [ ] useState / useEffect — FALSE, only Client Components
  • [x] Keep sensitive credentials out of the client bundle
  • [ ] onClick — FALSE, only Client Components
  • [x] Fetch data with async/await at component level
  • [ ] localStorage — FALSE, browser-only

Question 9 — Written Answer

A team-mate imports a Server Component inside a Client Component and gets a build error. Explain why this happens and describe two ways to resolve it.

▸ Show Answer

Why: When a Server Component is imported inside a "use client" module, it is pulled across the client boundary and becomes part of the client bundle. Server Components can contain server-only code (Node APIs, secrets, async DB calls) that cannot run in the browser, so Next.js throws an error.

Resolution 1 — Pass as children prop: Keep the Server Component in the parent (server) layout and pass it into the Client Component as children or another prop. The Server Component stays on the server; only its rendered output is sent to the client.

Resolution 2 — Move the Server Component higher: Restructure so the Client Component is a leaf and the Server Component wraps or sits alongside it rather than being imported by it.

Question 10 — Multiple Choice

What does the server-only npm package do when imported in a module?

  • A) It polyfills Node.js APIs for the browser environment
  • B) It throws a build-time error if the module is accidentally imported in a Client Component
  • C) It automatically adds "use server" to every export in the file
  • D) It disables tree-shaking for that module
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: Importing server-only at the top of a module causes Next.js to emit a build error if that module ends up in the client bundle, acting as a guard for server-only utilities (e.g., database helpers, secrets).

SECTION C: Data Fetching, Caching & Rendering Strategies

Question 11 — Fill in the Blanks

Complete the following fetch option descriptions:

1. fetch(url, { cache: '________' }) — never caches; always fetches fresh data (equivalent to SSR).
2. fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: ________ } }) — revalidates the cached response after N seconds (ISR / time-based revalidation).
3. fetch(url, { cache: '________' }) — caches indefinitely (default behaviour; equivalent to SSG).
4. fetch(url, { next: { tags: ['________'] } }) — tags the cache entry so it can be purged on demand.

▸ Show Answer

1. no-store

2. A number, e.g. 60 (seconds)

3. force-cache

4. A tag string, e.g. 'posts'

Question 12 — Multiple Choice

You want to purge all cached fetch calls tagged with 'products' after a product is updated via a Server Action. Which Next.js function do you call?

  • A) revalidateTag('products')
  • B) clearCache('products')
  • C) revalidatePath('/products')
  • D) unstable_cache.clear('products')
▸ Show Answer

Answer: A

Explanation: revalidateTag(tag) (from next/cache) invalidates all cached fetch requests that were tagged with that string. revalidatePath(path) also exists but invalidates by route path, not by tag. Both can only be called in Server Actions or Route Handlers.

Question 13 — Written Answer

Compare SSR, SSG, ISR, and CSR in Next.js. For each strategy, state: (a) when the HTML is generated, (b) the corresponding fetch cache option, and (c) the ideal use-case.

▸ Show Answer
StrategyWhen HTML is generatedCache optionIdeal use-case
SSGBuild timeforce-cache (default)Marketing pages, docs, content that rarely changes
ISRBuild time + background revalidation{ next: { revalidate: N } }Blog posts, product pages — fresh enough but still cached
SSRPer request on the serverno-storePersonalised pages, real-time data (dashboards, carts)
CSRIn the browser after JS loadsClient-side fetch (e.g., SWR, React Query)Highly interactive widgets, data that doesn't need SEO

Question 14 — Multiple Choice

What does generateStaticParams do in a dynamic route like app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx?

  • A) It validates URL params at runtime to prevent 404s
  • B) It returns an array of param objects so Next.js pre-renders those routes at build time (SSG)
  • C) It restricts which slugs are allowed in production
  • D) It generates TypeScript types for the params prop
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: generateStaticParams is the App Router replacement for getStaticPaths. It exports an async function that returns an array like [{ slug: 'hello' }, { slug: 'world' }]. Next.js renders each combination as a static HTML file at build time. Routes not in the list can still be served on demand (depending on dynamicParams setting).

Question 15 — Multiple Choice

How does loading.tsx create a streaming experience in Next.js App Router?

  • A) It uses a Web Worker to fetch data in the background
  • B) Next.js automatically wraps the route segment in a React <Suspense> boundary, sending the loading UI immediately while the async page resolves
  • C) It defers all JavaScript until after the first paint
  • D) It enables HTTP/2 server push for the page assets
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: When you create a loading.tsx file, Next.js wraps the corresponding page.tsx (and its layout children) in a <Suspense> boundary automatically. The loading UI is streamed to the browser immediately, and the actual page content replaces it once the async work is done.

Question 16 — Written Answer

Explain how you would use <Suspense> manually to stream parts of a page independently, without relying on loading.tsx. Write a short example.

▸ Show Answer

Wrap any async Server Component in <Suspense fallback={...}>. The fallback renders instantly while the async component resolves on the server and is then streamed in.

// app/dashboard/page.tsx (Server Component)
import { Suspense } from 'react'
import RevenueChart from './RevenueChart'
import RecentOrders from './RecentOrders'

export default function DashboardPage() {
  return (
    <main>
      <h1>Dashboard</h1>
      <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading chart...</p>}>
        <RevenueChart />
      </Suspense>
      <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading orders...</p>}>
        <RecentOrders />
      </Suspense>
    </main>
  )
}

Each component fetches its own data in parallel. Whichever resolves first is streamed to the client first, reducing time-to-interactive.

SECTION D: Server Actions, Metadata & Middleware

Question 17 — Multiple Choice

Where must the "use server" directive appear to define a Server Action?

  • A) Only at the top of a page.tsx file
  • B) At the top of a separate file (making all exports Server Actions), or at the top of an async function body inside a Server Component
  • C) Inside a "use client" component above the function body
  • D) In next.config.js under the serverActions key
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: "use server" can be placed at the top of a file (file-level directive, every exported async function becomes a Server Action) or at the top of an individual async function inside a Server Component. It cannot appear inside a Client Component function directly — instead, you import Server Actions from a "use server" file into Client Components.

Question 18 — Written Answer

Explain what useActionState (formerly useFormState) and useFormStatus do in the context of Server Actions. What are the rules about where each can be used?

▸ Show Answer

useActionState(action, initialState) — a React hook that wraps a Server Action. It returns [state, formAction, isPending]. state holds the value returned by the last action invocation. Must be used in a Client Component ("use client"). Enables progressive enhancement — the form works even without JS.

useFormStatus() — returns { pending, data, method, action } about the nearest parent <form>'s submission. Must be used in a Client Component that is a child of the form, not the form component itself. Typically used in a separate <SubmitButton> component to disable/show a spinner while the action is pending.

Question 19 — Multiple Choice

You want to define static metadata for a page in the App Router. Which is the correct approach?

  • A) Export a Head component from page.tsx
  • B) Export a metadata object from page.tsx or layout.tsx
  • C) Add a <meta> tag inside the JSX of the page
  • D) Use next/head inside the component
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: In the App Router, metadata is exported as a named metadata constant or via an async generateMetadata function from any page.tsx or layout.tsx. Next.js collects metadata from all segments and merges them. The old next/head pattern is from the Pages Router.

// Static
export const metadata = { title: 'My Page', description: '...' }

// Dynamic
export async function generateMetadata({ params }) {
  const product = await fetchProduct(params.id)
  return { title: product.name }
}

Question 20 — Written Answer

What is Next.js Middleware and where does the middleware.ts file live? Describe two practical use-cases and explain what NextResponse.redirect() vs NextResponse.rewrite() do.

▸ Show Answer

Location: middleware.ts goes at the root of the project (same level as app/ or pages/), not inside app/.

What it does: Runs on the Edge runtime before a request reaches the route handler/page, allowing inspection and transformation of requests and responses.

Use-cases:

  • Authentication — check a session cookie; redirect unauthenticated users to /login.
  • Internationalisation — detect the user's locale from Accept-Language header and rewrite the URL to /en/....

NextResponse.redirect(url) — sends a 3xx response to the client; the browser navigates to the new URL (URL changes in address bar).

NextResponse.rewrite(url) — serves content from a different URL while keeping the original URL in the address bar (transparent proxy).

SECTION E: Redux & Redux Toolkit

Question 21 — Fill in the Blanks

Complete the three core principles of Redux:

1. ________ — the entire application state is stored in a single object tree within one store.
2. ________ — state is read-only; the only way to change state is to dispatch an action (a plain object).
3. ________ — changes are made with pure functions called reducers that take (state, action) and return a new state.

▸ Show Answer

1. Single source of truth

2. State is read-only

3. Changes are made with pure functions (reducers)

Question 22 — Multiple Choice

Which Redux Toolkit function replaces the need to write action creators, action type strings, and a reducer switch statement manually?

  • A) configureStore
  • B) createSlice
  • C) createAction
  • D) createReducer
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: createSlice accepts a name, initialState, and a reducers object. It auto-generates action creators and action type strings, and produces a reducer — eliminating all the boilerplate. configureStore sets up the store with good defaults (Redux DevTools, redux-thunk middleware). createAction and createReducer are lower-level helpers used individually.

Question 23 — Written Answer

Explain how Redux Toolkit uses Immer inside createSlice reducers. Why is it safe to write state.value += 1 in a reducer even though Redux requires immutable updates?

▸ Show Answer

RTK integrates Immer into every reducer created by createSlice (and createReducer). Immer wraps the state in a Proxy object. When you mutate the proxy (e.g., state.value += 1), Immer tracks all changes and produces a new immutable state snapshot behind the scenes — the original state object is never actually mutated.

This means you can write straightforward "mutable-style" code in your reducers while Redux still receives a brand-new state reference, triggering proper re-renders. You must either mutate the proxy or return a new value — not both.

Question 24 — Multiple Choice

What does createAsyncThunk automatically dispatch around an async operation?

  • A) Only a fulfilled action when the promise resolves
  • B) Three actions: pending, fulfilled, and rejected
  • C) A single loading action followed by a done action
  • D) Nothing automatically — you dispatch actions manually inside the thunk
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: createAsyncThunk('slice/action', async (arg) => ...) generates three action creators: action.pending, action.fulfilled, and action.rejected. These are dispatched automatically at the start, on success, and on error respectively. You handle them in extraReducers inside createSlice to update loading state, data, and error fields.

Question 25 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following are features provided by RTK Query?

  • [ ] Automatic caching and cache invalidation via tags
  • [ ] Generated React hooks like useGetPostsQuery and useAddPostMutation
  • [ ] Polling support with pollingInterval
  • [ ] Built-in pagination using createPaginatedQuery
  • [ ] Optimistic updates via onQueryStarted
  • [ ] Automatic store setup (no configureStore needed)
▸ Show Answer

Correct:

  • [x] Automatic caching and cache invalidation via tags
  • [x] Generated React hooks (useGetPostsQuery, useAddPostMutation)
  • [x] Polling with pollingInterval
  • [ ] createPaginatedQuery — FALSE, pagination is done manually via query args
  • [x] Optimistic updates via onQueryStarted
  • [ ] Automatic store setup — FALSE, you still call configureStore and add the API slice's reducer and middleware

Question 26 — Written Answer

Describe the unidirectional data flow in Redux. What are the four steps from a user interaction to a UI update?

▸ Show Answer
  1. Event / User interaction — e.g., a button click in the UI triggers a call to dispatch(action).
  2. Dispatch — the action (a plain object with a type field and optional payload) is sent to the Redux store.
  3. Reducer — the store calls the root reducer with (currentState, action). The reducer is a pure function; it returns a new state without mutating the old one.
  4. Store update + re-render — the store saves the new state. React components subscribed via useSelector receive the new state and re-render with the updated data.

SECTION F: Context API & Zustand

Question 27 — Multiple Choice

What is the main performance pitfall when using React Context for frequently-changing state?

  • A) Context values are stored in the DOM and reading them is slow
  • B) Every consumer of the context re-renders whenever the context value changes, even if it only uses part of the value
  • C) Context does not work with concurrent React features
  • D) Context values cannot hold objects, only primitives
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: React Context has no built-in selector mechanism. Any time the context value object reference changes, all consumers re-render — even those that only use an unrelated slice of the value. This makes Context unsuitable for high-frequency state (e.g., mouse position, real-time data). It is best suited for low-frequency, globally-shared state like theme or authentication.

Question 28 — Written Answer

Compare the Zustand useStore selector pattern to naive Context usage. Why do Zustand selectors prevent unnecessary re-renders?

▸ Show Answer

Zustand's useStore(selector) hook subscribes a component only to the slice of state returned by the selector function. Zustand performs a shallow (or strict equality) comparison between the previous and next selector result. If the result is the same, the component is not re-rendered, even if other parts of the store changed.

// Only re-renders when bears count changes
const bears = useStore((state) => state.bears)

// vs Context: re-renders whenever ANY value in the provider changes

Context consumers re-render on every provider value change because React only does a reference equality check on the entire context value. Zustand solves this with per-component, fine-grained subscriptions — similar to Redux's useSelector.

Question 29 — Multiple Choice

Which Zustand middleware persists the store to localStorage (or another storage) and rehydrates it on page load?

  • A) devtools
  • B) immer
  • C) persist
  • D) subscribeWithSelector
▸ Show Answer

Answer: C

Explanation: The persist middleware from zustand/middleware automatically serialises the store to a storage engine (default: localStorage) and restores it when the app reloads. You can configure which keys to persist with the partialize option.

Question 30 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following are valid reasons to choose Zustand over Redux Toolkit?

  • [ ] Zustand requires no store provider wrapping the app
  • [ ] Zustand has significantly less boilerplate for simple global state
  • [ ] Zustand includes a built-in devtools extension with time-travel debugging by default
  • [ ] Zustand supports React Server Components out of the box
  • [ ] Zustand is better suited for extremely complex state with many interconnected slices managed by a large team
  • [ ] Zustand's tiny bundle size makes it attractive for small to medium apps
▸ Show Answer
  • [x] No provider wrapping needed — Zustand uses a module-level store
  • [x] Less boilerplate for simple use-cases
  • [ ] Built-in devtools by default — FALSE, you opt in via the devtools middleware
  • [ ] Supports RSC out of the box — FALSE, Zustand is client-side state; it does not run in Server Components
  • [ ] Better for large team / complex state — FALSE, Redux's strict patterns and middleware ecosystem scale better in those scenarios
  • [x] Tiny bundle size (~1 KB) is attractive for smaller apps

Question 31 — Multiple Choice

When is the React Context API the most appropriate tool for shared state, compared to Redux or Zustand?

  • A) When state changes very frequently, such as tracking mouse movement
  • B) When you need time-travel debugging
  • C) When the state is low-frequency and widely needed, such as the current theme, locale, or authenticated user
  • D) When you need optimistic updates in a large e-commerce app
▸ Show Answer

Answer: C

Explanation: Context shines for stable, app-wide values (theme, locale, auth). Because it re-renders all consumers on value change, using it for high-frequency updates causes performance problems. For complex or frequently-updated state, Redux Toolkit or Zustand are better choices.

SECTION G: react-hook-form

Question 32 — Multiple Choice

Why does react-hook-form (RHF) use uncontrolled inputs by default rather than controlled inputs?

  • A) Browsers do not support controlled inputs
  • B) Uncontrolled inputs avoid re-rendering the component on every keystroke, resulting in better performance
  • C) Uncontrolled inputs are required for server-side rendering
  • D) useState cannot store string values
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: In a controlled input pattern, every keystroke calls setState, which triggers a re-render of the whole form. RHF registers inputs with a ref (register), reads values from the DOM directly, and only triggers re-renders when necessary (e.g., on validation or submit). This makes large forms dramatically faster.

Question 33 — Fill in the Blanks

Complete the descriptions of key useForm return values:

1. ________ — a function that connects an input to RHF (returns ref, name, onChange, onBlur).
2. ________ — wraps your submit callback; calls validation before invoking your handler.
3. ________ — an object containing errors, isSubmitting, isDirty, isValid, etc.
4. ________ — programmatically sets the value of a field.
5. ________ — resets the form to default values (or a new set of values).

▸ Show Answer

1. register

2. handleSubmit

3. formState

4. setValue

5. reset

Question 34 — Written Answer

When should you use the Controller component from react-hook-form, and how does it work? Give an example with a UI library input.

▸ Show Answer

When to use: Controller is needed for controlled third-party components that do not expose a native ref (e.g., custom dropdowns, date-pickers from MUI, Radix UI selects, or React-Select). These components require value and onChange props rather than a DOM ref.

Controller acts as a bridge: it wraps the component, subscribes to RHF's store, and passes field.value, field.onChange, and field.onBlur to the inner component.

import { useForm, Controller } from 'react-hook-form'
import Select from 'react-select'

function MyForm() {
  const { control, handleSubmit } = useForm()

  return (
    <form onSubmit={handleSubmit(console.log)}>
      <Controller
        name="country"
        control={control}
        rules={{ required: true }}
        render={({ field }) => (
          <Select
            {...field}
            options={[{ value: 'is', label: 'Iceland' }]}
          />
        )}
      />
    </form>
  )
}

Question 35 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following are valid built-in validation rules for the register function in react-hook-form?

  • [ ] required
  • [ ] minLength
  • [ ] pattern
  • [ ] validate (custom function)
  • [ ] type
  • [ ] max / min (for numeric inputs)
  • [ ] unique
▸ Show Answer
  • [x] required
  • [x] minLength (also maxLength)
  • [x] pattern — RegExp
  • [x] validate — custom function or object of functions
  • [ ] type — FALSE, not a built-in rule (type is an HTML attribute)
  • [x] max / min — for numeric and date inputs
  • [ ] unique — FALSE, not built-in; implement with validate

Question 36 — Multiple Choice

You want to integrate Zod for schema validation with react-hook-form. Which package provides the resolver glue between them?

  • A) zod-form
  • B) @hookform/resolvers
  • C) react-zod-form
  • D) No extra package — RHF supports Zod natively
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: @hookform/resolvers provides adapters for Zod, Yup, Joi, Valibot, and others. You pass resolver: zodResolver(schema) to useForm. RHF then runs the Zod schema on submit (and optionally on change/blur) and maps errors back to the formState.errors object.

import { zodResolver } from '@hookform/resolvers/zod'
import { z } from 'zod'

const schema = z.object({
  email: z.string().email(),
  age: z.number().min(18),
})

const { register, handleSubmit, formState: { errors } } = useForm({
  resolver: zodResolver(schema),
})

Question 37 — Written Answer

Explain the difference between watch and getValues in react-hook-form. When should you use each?

▸ Show Answer

watch(fieldName) — subscribes the component to re-render whenever the watched field changes. Use it when the UI needs to react to a field value in real-time, e.g., showing a conditional section when a checkbox is ticked or computing a live preview.

getValues(fieldName?) — reads the current value(s) from the form store without subscribing to updates and without triggering a re-render. Use it inside event handlers or effects where you just need to read the value once (e.g., "copy shipping to billing" button, validating one field relative to another in a validate function).
