Rule of thumb: if you need the value in JSX / render output → watch. If you need it in a callback → getValues.

Question 38 — Multiple Choice

After a successful form submission you want to reset all fields to their original default values. Which RHF call achieves this?

  • A) clearErrors()
  • B) setValue('', '') for each field
  • C) reset() or reset(newDefaults)
  • D) unregister() all fields
▸ Show Answer

Answer: C

Explanation: reset() resets values, errors, and dirty/touched state back to defaultValues. You can also pass a new values object to reset(newValues) to set new defaults — useful when populating an edit form from a server response. clearErrors() only clears validation errors, it does not reset field values.

Question 39 — Written Answer

You have a form with a field named email. Describe how to display a custom error message for (a) a required error, (b) a pattern error, and (c) a server-side error returned after submission.

▸ Show Answer

Setup:

const { register, handleSubmit, formState: { errors }, setError } = useForm()

<input {...register('email', {
  required: 'Email is required',
  pattern: { value: /^\S+@\S+$/, message: 'Invalid email format' }
})} />

{errors.email && <p>{errors.email.message}</p>}

(a) Required error: When the field is empty, errors.email.type === 'required' and errors.email.message === 'Email is required'.

(b) Pattern error: When the value does not match the regex, errors.email.type === 'pattern' and errors.email.message === 'Invalid email format'.

(c) Server-side error: After a failed API call in the submit handler, call setError('email', { type: 'server', message: 'This email is already registered' }). RHF stores it in errors.email and the same {errors.email.message} JSX renders it.

Question 40 — Select All That Apply

Which statements about formState in react-hook-form are correct?

  • [ ] isDirty is true if any field value differs from its defaultValue
  • [ ] isSubmitting is true while the handleSubmit async callback is running
  • [ ] isValid reflects whether all validations currently pass (requires mode other than 'onSubmit' for live accuracy)
  • [ ] errors is populated as soon as the form mounts, before any user interaction
  • [ ] touchedFields tracks which fields the user has interacted with and blurred
  • [ ] submitCount tracks how many times the form has been submitted
▸ Show Answer
  • [x] isDirty — correct
  • [x] isSubmitting — correct
  • [x] isValid — correct (in 'onSubmit' mode it only updates after a submit attempt; use mode: 'onChange' or 'onBlur' for live validation)
  • [ ] errors populated on mount — FALSE, errors appear after validation triggers (by default, on submit)
  • [x] touchedFields — correct
  • [x] submitCount — correct

SECTION H: Mixed / Applied Questions

Question 41 — Written Answer

You are building a Next.js App Router application. Describe a complete architecture decision process: when would you reach for (a) Server Component data fetching, (b) RTK Query, (c) Zustand, and (d) react-hook-form, and how can they coexist?

▸ Show Answer

(a) Server Component data fetching — for any data needed to render the initial page that does not require user interaction or real-time updates (product listings, blog posts, user profile on a settings page). Fetch directly in the Server Component; no client-side state library needed.

(b) RTK Query — for data that must be fetched and mutated from the client side, especially when multiple components need the same cached data (e.g., a shopping cart, notifications badge, or any CRUD UI). RTK Query handles loading/error/cache states automatically.

(c) Zustand — for lightweight client-side UI state that doesn't map directly to server data: modal open/close, sidebar state, wizard step, or cross-component ephemeral state that doesn't warrant the overhead of Redux.

(d) react-hook-form — for all form state: field values, validation errors, dirty/touched tracking, submission state. RHF keeps form state out of React state and out of global stores — it is self-contained per form instance.

Coexistence example: A "Create Order" page uses a Server Component to fetch product options. A Client Component renders the form (RHF). On submit, the form calls an RTK Query mutation to POST the order. On success, Zustand updates a global notification store.

Question 42 — Multiple Choice

In a Next.js Route Handler (route.ts), you export a POST function. What is the correct return type for a JSON response?

  • A) return { json: data }
  • B) return Response.json(data) or return NextResponse.json(data)
  • C) return new HTMLResponse(data)
  • D) res.json(data) (Express-style)
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: App Router Route Handlers use the standard Web Response API. Response.json(data, { status: 200 }) or the Next.js helper NextResponse.json(data, { status: 201 }) both work. Express-style res.json() does not exist in the App Router.

// app/api/products/route.ts
export async function POST(request: Request) {
  const body = await request.json()
  const product = await db.create(body)
  return Response.json(product, { status: 201 })
}

Question 43 — Written Answer

A colleague adds "use client" to every component "just to be safe". Explain the performance implications and describe the correct strategy for deciding where to place the client boundary.

▸ Show Answer

Performance implications:

  • Every "use client" component (and everything it imports) ships to the browser as JavaScript, inflating the client bundle.
  • Server Components have zero JavaScript cost on the client — they render on the server and send HTML. Making everything a Client Component eliminates this advantage.
  • Server-side data fetching, direct DB access, and secret keys are also lost — they cannot live in Client Components.

Correct strategy — push the boundary to the leaves:

  1. Start with Server Components by default.
  2. Only add "use client" to the smallest component that actually needs interactivity (hooks, event handlers, browser APIs).
  3. Keep data-fetching and layout at the server level; pass data down as props to small client leaves.
  4. Or use the children composition pattern to thread Server Component output through a Client Component wrapper.

Question 44 — Multiple Choice

You call revalidatePath('/blog') inside a Server Action. What exactly gets invalidated?

  • A) Only the /blog page's HTML cache
  • B) The cached data and rendered output associated with the /blog route (and optionally its sub-paths, depending on the type parameter)
  • C) All routes in the entire application
  • D) Only the fetch requests that were made on /blog
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: revalidatePath(path, type?) purges the router cache and Data Cache for the specified path. The optional type argument can be 'page' (default, exact path) or 'layout' (invalidates the path and all routes that share its layout). It does not affect every route in the app.

Question 45 — Written Answer

Explain how to implement optimistic UI updates using RTK Query's onQueryStarted lifecycle. Walk through the rollback pattern if the server request fails.

▸ Show Answer

RTK Query mutations expose onQueryStarted(arg, { dispatch, queryFulfilled, getState }) in the endpoint definition. You can patch the cache optimistically before the request completes:

updatePost: builder.mutation({
  query: (patch) => ({ url: `/posts/${patch.id}`, method: 'PATCH', body: patch }),
  async onQueryStarted({ id, ...patch }, { dispatch, queryFulfilled }) {
    // 1. Optimistically update the cache
    const patchResult = dispatch(
      postsApi.util.updateQueryData('getPosts', undefined, (draft) => {
        const post = draft.find((p) => p.id === id)
        if (post) Object.assign(post, patch)
      })
    )
    try {
      // 2. Wait for the real response
      await queryFulfilled
    } catch {
      // 3. Roll back on failure
      patchResult.undo()
    }
  },
})

The updateQueryData utility (powered by Immer) creates an optimistic patch and returns an undo function. If queryFulfilled rejects, calling patchResult.undo() reverses the optimistic change, restoring the previous cache state.

Question 46 — Multiple Choice

In react-hook-form, what is the default validation trigger (mode)?

  • A) 'onChange' — validates on every keystroke
  • B) 'onBlur' — validates when the field loses focus
  • C) 'onSubmit' — validates only when the form is submitted
  • D) 'all' — validates on change, blur, and submit
▸ Show Answer

Answer: C

Explanation: The default mode is 'onSubmit'. Errors only appear after the first submit attempt. After the first submit, RHF switches to onChange re-validation for already-errored fields. You can override with useForm({ mode: 'onBlur' }), 'onChange', or 'all'.

Question 47 — Written Answer

Describe the complete flow of a form submission using a Next.js Server Action without any client-side JavaScript (progressive enhancement). What must be true for this to work?

▸ Show Answer

Flow:

  1. The form has a native HTML <form action={serverAction}>. No onSubmit JS handler needed.
  2. When the user submits, the browser serialises the form fields as multipart/form-data and POSTs to the Next.js Server Action endpoint.
  3. Next.js invokes the Server Action with a FormData object on the server.
  4. The action reads data via formData.get('fieldName'), validates, and performs the mutation.
  5. The server re-renders the page with updated state (or calls redirect()).

Requirements for progressive enhancement:

  • Use a real <form> element with a native submit button (<button type="submit">).
  • Pass the Server Action to the action attribute, not onSubmit.
  • The action must be defined with "use server".
  • All form fields must have name attributes so they appear in FormData.

When JS is available, React intercepts the submit and calls the action without a full page reload (enhanced behaviour). Without JS, the form still works via standard browser form submission.

Question 48 — Multiple Choice

You have a createSlice with a reducer that returns a new state object instead of mutating the Immer draft. Which of the following is true?

  • A) This is forbidden — you must always mutate the draft
  • B) This is valid — you can either mutate the draft OR return a new value, but not both
  • C) Returning a new value from a reducer causes a TypeScript error in RTK
  • D) Immer ignores returned values and always uses the mutated draft
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: Immer supports two patterns in a reducer: (1) mutate the proxy draft directly and return nothing, or (2) return a completely new value (bypassing Immer). You cannot do both — if you mutate the draft AND return a new value, Immer throws an error. Returning a new value is useful when you want to replace the entire state slice (e.g., resetting to initial state).

// Pattern 1 — mutate draft (Immer handles immutability)
increment(state) { state.count += 1 }

// Pattern 2 — return new value
reset() { return initialState }

// INVALID — do not do both
broken(state) { state.count += 1; return { count: 0 } }

Question 49 — Written Answer

What is the difference between generateStaticParams and generateMetadata in Next.js App Router? Can they both be exported from the same file?

▸ Show Answer

generateStaticParams — exported from a dynamic page.tsx (e.g., app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx). Returns an array of params objects that Next.js should pre-render as static pages at build time. Replaces getStaticPaths from the Pages Router.

generateMetadata — exported from any page.tsx or layout.tsx. Returns a Metadata object (title, description, OpenGraph, etc.) optionally after fetching data. Replaces the static metadata export when the metadata depends on route params or remote data. Replaces getServerSideProps-based head injection from the Pages Router.

Can they coexist? Yes. Both can be exported from the same page.tsx file. Next.js deduplicates any shared fetch calls between them automatically using the built-in request memoisation (React's cache).

Question 50 — Multiple Choice

You need to access a user's authentication token stored in a cookie inside a Next.js Server Component. Which API do you use?

  • A) document.cookie
  • B) cookies() from 'next/headers'
  • C) getServerSideProps context
  • D) useContext(CookieContext)
▸ Show Answer

Answer: B

Explanation: Next.js provides cookies() (and headers()) from 'next/headers' for reading incoming request cookies/headers in Server Components, Server Actions, and Route Handlers. document.cookie is browser-only. getServerSideProps is Pages Router only. Hooks like useContext cannot run in Server Components.

import { cookies } from 'next/headers'

export default async function ProfilePage() {
  const token = cookies().get('auth-token')?.value
  const user = await fetchUser(token)
  return <div>{user.name}</div>
}

Bonus Questions — Socket.io, Web APIs, Security & More

SECTION A: SOCKET.IO — ADVANCED

Question 1 — Fill in the blanks

Complete the following sentences about Socket.io server setup and namespaces:

A Socket.io namespace is created with 1. ________ and acts like a separate communication channel over the same underlying connection. The default namespace is 2. ________. On the client side you connect to a custom namespace by passing the namespace path to 3. ________. Within a namespace, 4. ________ are sub-groups that let you target a subset of connected sockets. Calling io.to(room).emit() on the server sends the event to 5. ________ in that room.

▸ Show Answer

1. io.of('/namespaceName') (e.g. io.of('/chat'))

2. / (the root namespace)

3. io('http://server/namespaceName') (e.g. io('/chat'))

4. rooms

5. all sockets (all clients)

Question 2 — Multiple Choice

What is the difference between io.to(room).emit() and socket.to(room).emit() on the server?

  • A) They are identical — both send to all sockets in the room including the sender
  • B) io.to(room).emit() includes the sender; socket.to(room).emit() excludes the sender
  • C) socket.to(room).emit() can only be used inside a connection handler; io.to(room).emit() can be used anywhere
  • D) Both B and C are correct
▸ Show Answer

D — Both statements are true. io.to(room).emit() sends to all sockets in the room (including the sender if they are in the room). socket.to(room).emit() sends to everyone in the room except the socket it is called on. Additionally, socket.to() only has meaning inside a connection/event handler because it references a specific socket context, while io.to() is accessible from any scope as long as you have the io instance.

Question 3 — Code Analysis (6 pts)

Read the following Socket.io server code and answer the questions below:

io.on('connection', (socket) => {
  socket.on('joinRoom', (roomId, callback) => {
    socket.join(roomId);
    callback({ status: 'ok', room: roomId });
  });

  socket.on('chatMessage', ({ roomId, text }) => {
    socket.to(roomId).emit('newMessage', { text, from: socket.id });
  });

  socket.on('disconnect', (reason) => {
    console.log(`Socket ${socket.id} disconnected: ${reason}`);
  });
});

a) What is the purpose of the third argument (callback) in the joinRoom handler, and what Socket.io feature does this demonstrate? (2 pts)

b) When a client emits chatMessage, who receives the resulting newMessage event? (1 pt)

c) Name two common values the reason string might hold in the disconnect handler and what each means. (2 pts)

▸ Show Answer

a) callback demonstrates Socket.io acknowledgements. After joining the room, the server invokes the callback function with a response object — the client receives this response as confirmation. The client would emit like: socket.emit('joinRoom', roomId, (response) => console.log(response)). This gives a one-shot request/response pattern on top of the event model — no need to emit a separate "joinRoomSuccess" event.

b) Only the sockets in roomId except the sender receive newMessage. This uses socket.to(roomId) which excludes the emitting socket.

c) Common reasons:
"transport close" — the underlying transport (WebSocket/polling) was closed, typically because the client closed the browser tab or lost the connection.
"server namespace disconnect" — the server explicitly called socket.disconnect() to force the client off.
Other valid answers: "transport error" (network error), "ping timeout" (client stopped responding to heartbeats).

Question 4 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following statements about Socket.io namespaces are correct?

  • [ ] Namespaces share the same underlying TCP connection when using WebSocket transport
  • [ ] A client can be connected to multiple namespaces at the same time
  • [ ] Rooms and namespaces are the same thing — just different names
  • [ ] Middleware registered on one namespace does not affect other namespaces
  • [ ] You can emit to an entire namespace with io.of('/chat').emit('event', data)
  • [ ] Namespaces have no practical benefit over just using rooms
▸ Show Answer

Correct answers:

  • [x] Namespaces share the same underlying TCP connection — Socket.io multiplexes namespaces over one transport
  • [x] A client can connect to multiple namespaces simultaneously
  • [ ] Rooms and namespaces are the same — FALSE. Namespaces are top-level connection segregation with separate middleware/event listeners. Rooms are lightweight sub-groups within a namespace.
  • [x] Middleware is per-namespace
  • [x] io.of('/chat').emit() broadcasts to all sockets in that namespace
  • [ ] No practical benefit — FALSE. Namespaces allow separate authorization rules, separate event spaces, and cleaner code separation.

Question 5 — Written Answer (4 pts)

Explain how Socket.io reconnection works by default. What client-side options can you configure to control reconnection behavior? Give two examples with their effect.

▸ Show Answer

By default, Socket.io clients automatically attempt to reconnect when the connection is lost. The reconnection strategy uses exponential backoff — it waits an increasing amount of time between attempts to avoid overwhelming the server.

Configurable options (set on client at connection time):

  • reconnection: false — disables automatic reconnection entirely. Useful if you want to handle reconnection logic yourself.
  • reconnectionAttempts: 5 — limits how many times the client tries before giving up. After exhausting attempts the client emits reconnect_failed.
  • reconnectionDelay: 1000 — initial delay (ms) before the first reconnection attempt.
  • reconnectionDelayMax: 5000 — caps the maximum delay between attempts.

During reconnection the client emits events: reconnecting (with attempt number), reconnect (on success), and reconnect_failed (on giving up). Servers should emit any missed state on connection because Socket.io does not replay missed events.

Question 6 — Multiple Choice

A client emits an event with an acknowledgement callback. The server never calls the callback. What happens on the client side by default?

  • A) The client throws an unhandled Promise rejection
  • B) The callback is never called and the client has no built-in timeout for it
  • C) Socket.io automatically retries the emit after 5 seconds
  • D) The connection is closed and reconnection is triggered
▸ Show Answer

B — By default there is no built-in timeout for acknowledgements. If the server never calls the callback, it simply never fires. You must implement your own timeout logic (e.g. with setTimeout) or use the timeout() helper added in Socket.io v4: socket.timeout(5000).emit('event', data, callback) which will call the callback with a timeout error after 5 seconds if the server hasn't responded.

Question 7 — Code Writing (5 pts)

Write a minimal Socket.io server setup in Node.js that:

  1. Creates an HTTP server and attaches Socket.io to it with CORS allowed for http://localhost:3000
  2. Uses middleware to reject connections that do not provide a token in socket.handshake.auth
  3. On connection, logs the socket ID
▸ Show Answer 
const http = require('http');
const { Server } = require('socket.io');

const server = http.createServer();
const io = new Server(server, {
  cors: {
    origin: 'http://localhost:3000',
    methods: ['GET', 'POST']
  }
});

// Middleware — runs before every connection completes
io.use((socket, next) => {
  const token = socket.handshake.auth.token;
  if (!token) {
    return next(new Error('Authentication error: no token'));
  }
  // Optionally verify the token here...
  next();
});

io.on('connection', (socket) => {
  console.log('Socket connected:', socket.id);
});

server.listen(3001, () => console.log('Listening on port 3001'));

Key points: new Server(httpServer, options) attaches Socket.io. CORS is configured in the constructor options. io.use() registers middleware — calling next(new Error(...)) rejects the connection and the client receives a connect_error event.

SECTION B: WEBSOCKETS VS HTTP VS SSE VS LONG POLLING

Question 8 — Multiple Choice

Which of the following best describes Server-Sent Events (SSE)?

  • A) A bidirectional full-duplex protocol that upgrades from HTTP/1.1
  • B) A unidirectional server-to-client push mechanism over a persistent HTTP connection
  • C) A polling strategy where the client sends a request every few seconds
  • D) A peer-to-peer protocol that bypasses the server entirely
▸ Show Answer

B — SSE (EventSource API) opens a persistent HTTP connection over which the server can push data to the client. It is unidirectional (server → client only). It uses standard HTTP/1.1 with Content-Type: text/event-stream. Built-in automatic reconnection is provided by the browser. For bidirectional communication, WebSockets are needed instead.

Question 9 — Select All That Apply

Compare the four real-time techniques. Which statements are correct?

  • [ ] WebSockets require an initial HTTP handshake (the "Upgrade" request) before switching protocols
  • [ ] Long polling keeps a request open on the server until data is ready, then immediately closes and the client re-opens
  • [ ] SSE supports bidirectional communication natively
  • [ ] WebSockets work over TCP and allow both the client and server to send messages at any time
  • [ ] HTTP polling sends a new request every N seconds regardless of whether new data exists
  • [ ] SSE automatically reconnects if the connection drops, using the Last-Event-ID header to resume
▸ Show Answer

Correct answers:

  • [x] WebSockets start with an HTTP Upgrade handshake
  • [x] Long polling: server holds the request open, responds when data arrives, client immediately re-issues
  • [ ] SSE is unidirectional (server → client only) — FALSE
  • [x] WebSockets are full-duplex over TCP
  • [x] HTTP polling sends on a timer regardless of new data — wasteful but simple
  • [x] SSE auto-reconnects using Last-Event-ID so the server can replay missed events

Question 10 — Written Answer (4 pts)

A product manager asks: "We need live score updates for a sports app. Users never need to send data back except for subscribing to a team." Compare WebSockets and SSE for this use case. Which would you recommend and why?

▸ Show Answer

Recommendation: SSE — because the data flow is unidirectional (server pushes scores, client only "subscribes" once via a URL parameter or query string). SSE fits perfectly here.

SSE advantages for this use case:

  • Simpler to implement — just a standard HTTP endpoint returning text/event-stream
  • Works through HTTP/2 multiplexing with no extra library
  • Built-in browser reconnection with Last-Event-ID resume
  • Easier load-balancer/proxy compatibility (plain HTTP)

When WebSockets would be better: if users could also chat, place bets, or interact in real time — i.e. when bidirectional communication is required.

SECTION C: HTTP METHODS, STATUS CODES & REST

Question 11 — Fill in the blanks

Complete the following sentences about HTTP:

A request is safe if it does not 1. ________. A request is idempotent if making the same request multiple times produces the 2. ________ result as making it once. GET, HEAD, and OPTIONS are both safe and idempotent. PUT is idempotent but 3. ________. POST is neither safe nor idempotent. The HTTP method used to apply a partial update to a resource is 4. ________.

▸ Show Answer

1. modify server state (has no side effects / does not alter the resource)

2. same server-state

3. not safe (it modifies/replaces the resource)

4. PATCH

Question 12 — Multiple Choice

An API endpoint receives a DELETE /users/42 request. The user with ID 42 does not exist. Which HTTP status code is most appropriate according to REST conventions?

  • A) 200 OK
  • B) 204 No Content
  • C) 404 Not Found
  • D) 400 Bad Request
▸ Show Answer

C — 404 Not Found is the conventional response when the resource to be deleted does not exist. Some APIs argue for returning 204 (idempotent — deleting a non-existent resource has the same outcome as deleting an existing one), but returning 404 is more common in practice to signal that the resource was never found. Both C and B are debated; the "correct" REST answer is usually 404 because the client should know the resource they targeted doesn't exist.

Question 13 — Match the Status Code

Match each HTTP status code with its correct meaning:

Status CodeOptions
201A) The server cannot find the requested resource
301B) The request succeeded and a new resource was created
304C) The resource has permanently moved to a new URL
401D) The client is not authenticated (no or invalid credentials)
403E) The server understood the request but refuses to authorize it
404F) The cached version is still valid; use the local copy
409G) Conflict — the request conflicts with the current state of the resource
422H) The request was well-formed but had semantic errors (validation failed)
429I) The client has sent too many requests in a given time (rate limited)
503J) The server is temporarily unable to handle the request
▸ Show Answer
  • 201 → B (Created)
  • 301 → C (Moved Permanently)
  • 304 → F (Not Modified — use cache)
  • 401 → D (Unauthorized — no/invalid credentials; implies "please authenticate")
  • 403 → E (Forbidden — credentials are present/valid but insufficient permissions)
  • 404 → A (Not Found)
  • 409 → G (Conflict)
  • 422 → H (Unprocessable Entity — validation error)
  • 429 → I (Too Many Requests)
  • 503 → J (Service Unavailable)

Question 14 — Written Answer (4 pts)

Explain the difference between 401 Unauthorized and 403 Forbidden. Give a concrete scenario for each.

▸ Show Answer

401 Unauthorized — despite its name, this means unauthenticated. The server does not know who is making the request (no credentials provided, or the provided token/session is invalid/expired). The HTTP spec requires the response to include a WWW-Authenticate header explaining how to authenticate. Scenario: A user tries to access GET /api/profile without sending an Authorization header at all — the server returns 401 saying "you need to log in first."

403 Forbidden — the server knows who the user is (they are authenticated) but the user does not have permission to access the resource. Scenario: A logged-in regular user tries to access DELETE /api/admin/users/5 — the server recognizes them but they are not an admin, so it returns 403 saying "you are not allowed to do this."

Question 15 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following are core REST constraints / principles?

  • [ ] Statelessness — each request must contain all information needed to process it; the server stores no client session state
  • [ ] REST requires the use of JSON as the data format
  • [ ] Uniform interface — resources are identified by URIs and manipulated through standard HTTP methods
  • [ ] Client–Server separation — UI concerns are decoupled from data storage concerns
  • [ ] REST APIs must always use HTTPS
  • [ ] Cacheability — responses must define whether they are cacheable to improve performance
  • [ ] Layered system — a client cannot tell whether it is connected directly to the end server or an intermediary
▸ Show Answer

Correct answers:

  • [x] Statelessness
  • [ ] JSON required — FALSE. REST is format-agnostic; XML, JSON, plain text, etc. are all valid.
  • [x] Uniform interface
  • [x] Client–Server separation
  • [ ] Must use HTTPS — FALSE. HTTPS is strongly recommended for security but is not part of the REST architectural constraints.
  • [x] Cacheability
  • [x] Layered system

SECTION D: CORS

Question 16 — Fill in the blanks

Complete the following sentences about CORS:

CORS stands for 1. ________. A CORS preflight request uses the HTTP method 2. ________. The server signals that a cross-origin request is allowed by returning the response header 3. ________. A request is considered "simple" (no preflight needed) only if it uses one of these methods: 4. ________ and uses only CORS-safelisted headers. When credentials (cookies) must be sent cross-origin, the client must set 5. ________ to true on the fetch/XHR, AND the server must respond with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true AND must NOT set Access-Control-Allow-Origin to 6. ________.

▸ Show Answer

1. Cross-Origin Resource Sharing

2. OPTIONS

3. Access-Control-Allow-Origin (with the allowed origin value)

4. GET, HEAD, or POST

5. credentials: 'include' (fetch) / withCredentials: true (XHR)

6. * (wildcard) — using * with credentials is forbidden by the spec; the exact origin must be echoed

Question 17 — Multiple Choice

A frontend at https://app.example.com sends a PUT request with a custom header X-Request-ID to https://api.example.com. The server has CORS configured correctly. What will the browser do before the actual PUT?

  • A) Nothing — PUT is a simple method and no preflight is needed
  • B) Send an OPTIONS preflight to https://api.example.com to check if PUT and X-Request-ID are allowed
  • C) Block the request entirely because cross-origin PUT is always forbidden
  • D) Send the PUT directly but strip the custom header for security
▸ Show Answer

B — A preflight is triggered because: (1) PUT is not a CORS-simple method (only GET, HEAD, POST are simple), and (2) X-Request-ID is not a CORS-safelisted request header. The browser sends OPTIONS with Access-Control-Request-Method: PUT and Access-Control-Request-Headers: x-request-id. Only if the server responds with the appropriate Access-Control-Allow-* headers will the actual PUT proceed.

Question 18 — Written Answer (4 pts)

A developer sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on their API. A frontend tries to send a request with credentials: 'include' (cookies). The request fails. Explain why, and describe exactly what changes must be made on both the server and client to fix it.

▸ Show Answer

Why it fails: The CORS spec explicitly prohibits the wildcard (*) for Access-Control-Allow-Origin when Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true is also needed. Browsers enforce this — using * with credentialed requests is treated as an error, and the browser blocks the response.

Server-side fixes:

  1. Change Access-Control-Allow-Origin from * to the exact origin: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com
  2. Add the header: Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
  3. Optionally add Vary: Origin so caches do not serve the wrong origin's response

Client-side fix: the fetch must already include credentials: 'include' (or withCredentials: true for XHR) — if this is already present, no additional client change is needed.

SECTION E: AUTHENTICATION — JWT, SESSIONS & COOKIES

Question 19 — Fill in the blanks

Complete the following sentences about JWTs:

A JWT consists of three parts separated by dots: 1. ________, 2. ________, and 3. ________. The payload is 4. ________ encoded (not encrypted), meaning it can be decoded by anyone. To verify that a JWT has not been tampered with, the recipient checks the 5. ________. A JWT stored in localStorage is accessible to JavaScript and therefore vulnerable to 6. ________ attacks.

▸ Show Answer

1. Header

2. Payload

3. Signature

4. Base64URL

5. Signature (using the secret key / public key)

6. XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)

Question 20 — Multiple Choice

Which cookie attribute makes a cookie inaccessible to JavaScript (i.e. document.cookie)?

  • A) Secure
  • B) SameSite=Strict
  • C) HttpOnly
  • D) Path=/
▸ Show Answer

C — HttpOnly prevents JavaScript from accessing the cookie via document.cookie. This is the primary defense for session cookies against XSS — even if an attacker injects a script, it cannot read or exfiltrate the HttpOnly cookie. Secure only ensures the cookie is sent over HTTPS. SameSite restricts cross-site sending (defense against CSRF).

Question 21 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following are true about the SameSite cookie attribute?

  • [ ] SameSite=Strict prevents the cookie from being sent on ANY cross-site request, including navigating to the site from a link
  • [ ] SameSite=Lax allows the cookie to be sent on top-level navigations (e.g. clicking a link) but not on sub-resource requests
  • [ ] SameSite=None can be used without the Secure attribute in modern browsers
  • [ ] The primary security purpose of SameSite is to mitigate CSRF attacks
  • [ ] If SameSite is not set, modern browsers default to SameSite=Lax
▸ Show Answer

Correct answers:

  • [x] Strict blocks the cookie even on top-level navigations from another site
  • [x] Lax allows top-level navigations (GET) but not cross-site sub-resource requests (AJAX, img, form POST)
  • [ ] SameSite=None without Secure — FALSE. Modern browsers require Secure when using SameSite=None; without it the cookie is rejected.
  • [x] SameSite mitigates CSRF
  • [x] Modern browsers (Chrome 80+) default unset cookies to Lax

Question 22 — Written Answer (5 pts)

Compare session-based authentication and JWT-based (token) authentication. Discuss: where state lives, scalability implications, and one security consideration for each.

▸ Show Answer

Session-based:

  • State location: Server stores session data (usually in memory or a database like Redis). The client holds only a session ID (typically in a cookie).
  • Scalability: Stateful — if the app runs on multiple servers, session store must be shared (e.g. Redis), or sticky sessions must be used. This adds infrastructure complexity.
  • Security consideration: Session IDs in cookies should use HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax. Session fixation and hijacking are risks if IDs are not rotated on login.

JWT-based:

  • State location: Stateless — all user info is encoded in the token itself. The server does not store anything per-user.
  • Scalability: Easier horizontal scaling — any server can validate the token using the shared secret/public key, no shared session store needed.
  • Security consideration: JWTs cannot be invalidated before expiry without a server-side token revocation list (effectively making them stateful). Storing JWTs in localStorage exposes them to XSS. Prefer HttpOnly cookies for JWT storage.

SECTION F: WEB STORAGE COMPARISON & SECURITY

Question 23 — Multiple Choice

Which storage mechanism is shared across all tabs and windows of the same origin AND persists after the browser is closed?

  • A) sessionStorage
  • B) localStorage
  • C) A non-HttpOnly cookie with no expiry
  • D) Both B and C
▸ Show Answer

D — Both localStorage and cookies without an expiry date (session cookies do expire, but cookies with an explicit Expires or Max-Age do not) persist after the browser is closed and are shared across tabs of the same origin. sessionStorage is per-tab and cleared when the tab closes.

Question 24 — Written Answer (4 pts)

Explain how a Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attack works, what data it can steal, and describe two complementary defenses.

▸ Show Answer

How XSS works: An attacker injects malicious JavaScript into a web page viewed by other users — either via stored input (stored XSS: comment saved to DB that renders unescaped), reflected input (reflected XSS: URL parameter echoed into HTML), or DOM manipulation (DOM XSS: client-side code writes unsanitized data to the DOM). The injected script runs in the victim's browser under the site's origin.

What it can steal: Anything accessible in the DOM or JavaScript context — localStorage, sessionStorage, non-HttpOnly cookies, form inputs, auth tokens, CSRF tokens, keystrokes.

Two defenses:

  1. Output encoding / escaping — HTML-encode user-supplied data before inserting it into HTML. Never use innerHTML with untrusted data; use textContent instead. Use templating libraries that auto-escape.
  2. Content Security Policy (CSP) — an HTTP response header (Content-Security-Policy) that tells the browser which sources of scripts are trusted. A strict CSP (e.g. script-src 'self') blocks inline scripts and external scripts from unauthorised domains, limiting the damage even if injection occurs.

Question 25 — Written Answer (4 pts)

Explain how a Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attack works. What makes it possible, and describe two mitigations.

▸ Show Answer

How CSRF works: The attacker crafts a malicious page that silently makes a state-changing HTTP request (e.g. POST /transfer-money) to a site where the victim is already logged in. Because the browser automatically attaches cookies for that origin, the server sees a request with valid session credentials and processes it — even though the user never intended to make the request.

What makes it possible: Cookies are sent automatically with every same-origin request from the browser, regardless of where the page initiating the request came from.

Two mitigations:

  1. SameSite cookie attribute — Setting SameSite=Lax or SameSite=Strict on session cookies prevents the browser from sending them on cross-origin requests, stopping most CSRF vectors.
  2. CSRF tokens (synchronizer token pattern) — the server embeds a unique, unpredictable token in forms/responses. The client must echo it back in subsequent requests (e.g. in a header or request body). An attacker on a different origin cannot read the token (blocked by CORS/SOP) and thus cannot forge a valid request.

SECTION G: ACCESSIBILITY

Question 26 — Fill in the blanks

Complete the following sentences about web accessibility:

ARIA stands for 1. ________. The ARIA attribute that gives an element an accessible name when no visible label exists is 2. ________. To indicate that a custom button is currently pressed/active, you use the ARIA attribute 3. ________. The tabindex="0" attribute makes an element 4. ________ in the natural tab order. Setting tabindex="-1" removes the element from the tab order but still allows it to be 5. ________ programmatically. The WCAG minimum contrast ratio for normal text (Level AA) is 6. ________.

▸ Show Answer

1. Accessible Rich Internet Applications

2. aria-label (or aria-labelledby)

3. aria-pressed

4. focusable / reachable by Tab key

5. focused (via element.focus())

6. 4.5:1

Question 27 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following are correct accessibility practices?

  • [ ] Using <div role="button" tabindex="0"> is exactly equivalent to using a native <button> element
  • [ ] All images must have an alt attribute, but decorative images should use alt=""
  • [ ] Keyboard users expect interactive elements to be operable with both Enter and Space keys for buttons
  • [ ] Color alone can be used to convey information as long as the contrast ratio is sufficient
  • [ ] The aria-live="polite" attribute causes a screen reader to announce content updates when the user is idle
  • [ ] Focus management is important in SPAs when navigating between views
▸ Show Answer

Correct answers:

  • [ ] div role="button" is exactly equivalent — FALSE. A native <button> gets keyboard handling, Enter/Space activation, and implicit ARIA role for free. A div requires manually adding keyboard listeners (keydown for Enter/Space) and is harder to maintain. Prefer native HTML.
  • [x] Decorative images use alt="" (empty string, not absent) so screen readers skip them
  • [x] Buttons should respond to both Enter and Space keys per ARIA authoring practices
  • [ ] Color alone — FALSE. WCAG 1.4.1 (Use of Color) requires that color is never the sole means of conveying information; additional cues (text, icons, patterns) must be used.
  • [x] aria-live="polite" announces when user is not actively typing
  • [x] SPAs must manage focus (e.g. move focus to the page heading on route change) so screen reader users know a navigation occurred

Question 28 — Written Answer (4 pts)

A developer builds a modal dialog with a <div> overlay. List four specific accessibility requirements a modal must fulfill for it to be usable by keyboard and screen reader users.

▸ Show Answer
  1. Focus trap — while the modal is open, Tab and Shift+Tab should cycle only through focusable elements inside the modal. Focus must not escape to the background page.
  2. Initial focus — when the modal opens, focus must be moved into it (typically to the first focusable element or the modal container with tabindex="-1").
  3. Return focus — when the modal closes, focus must return to the element that triggered it (e.g. the button that opened it).
  4. ARIA role and labeling — the modal container needs role="dialog" (or role="alertdialog" for critical messages), aria-modal="true", and an accessible name via aria-labelledby pointing to the modal's heading.
  5. Bonus: Escape key should close the modal.
  6. Bonus: Background content should be inert (inert attribute or aria-hidden="true") so screen readers cannot accidentally navigate to it.

SECTION H: PERFORMANCE

Question 29 — Multiple Choice

Which Core Web Vital measures the time until the largest visible content element (image, text block) has rendered on screen?

  • A) First Contentful Paint (FCP)
  • B) Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)
  • C) Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)
  • D) Interaction to Next Paint (INP)
▸ Show Answer

B — LCP measures how long until the largest content element in the viewport is rendered. A good LCP score is ≤2.5 seconds. The other Core Web Vitals: CLS measures visual stability (unexpected layout shifts), INP (replacing FID) measures responsiveness to user interactions.

Question 30 — Fill in the blanks

Complete the following sentences about React performance optimization:

React.memo prevents a component from re-rendering if its 1. ________ have not changed. The hook useMemo memoizes the 2. ________ of an expensive calculation between renders. The hook useCallback memoizes a 3. ________ reference so it does not change on every render. In Next.js, dynamic() enables 4. ________, which splits the bundle so the component is loaded only when needed. Adding loading="lazy" to an <img> tag is known as 5. ________ and defers loading until the image enters the viewport.

▸ Show Answer

1. props

2. return value / result

3. function

4. code splitting (dynamic/lazy imports)

5. lazy loading

Question 31 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following correctly describes when useMemo and useCallback should be used?

  • [ ] useMemo should be used for every computed value to prevent any wasted computation
  • [ ] useCallback is especially useful when passing callbacks to child components wrapped in React.memo
  • [ ] useMemo is appropriate when a calculation is genuinely expensive and its inputs change infrequently
  • [ ] Memoization has zero cost — it always makes components faster
  • [ ] useCallback(fn, [dep]) returns the same function reference as long as dep has not changed
▸ Show Answer

Correct answers:

  • [ ] useMemo everywhere — FALSE. Memoization has overhead (memory + comparison cost). Use it only when the computation is provably expensive.
  • [x] useCallback is useful with React.memo children — because without it, a new function reference is created each render, causing the memoized child to re-render anyway
  • [x] useMemo for expensive, infrequently-changing calculations
  • [ ] Zero cost — FALSE. React must store the cached value, run the dependency comparison, and manage memory. Premature memoization can be slower than no memoization for simple values.
  • [x] useCallback(fn, [dep]) returns a stable reference when deps are unchanged

SECTION I: TESTING

Question 32 — Fill in the blanks

Complete the following sentences about React Testing Library query methods:

Use 1. ________ when you expect the element to be in the DOM — it throws immediately if not found. Use 2. ________ when the element might not be in the DOM — it returns null instead of throwing. Use 3. ________ when you need to wait for an element that appears asynchronously — it returns a Promise and retries until the element appears or the timeout expires. The "by" variant that best mimics how users interact with a page (and is preferred by RTL authors) is 4. ________.

▸ Show Answer

1. getBy* (e.g. getByText, getByRole)

2. queryBy* (e.g. queryByText)

3. findBy* (e.g. findByText) — async, uses waitFor internally

4. ByRole — queries by ARIA role, which reflects how assistive technology sees the element and aligns with how users perceive the UI

Question 33 — Code Analysis (5 pts)

Review the following Jest test and answer the questions:

import { render, screen, fireEvent } from '@testing-library/react';
import Counter from './Counter';

test('increments count when button is clicked', () => {
  render(<Counter />);

  const button = screen.getByRole('button', { name: /increment/i });
  const display = screen.getByText(/count: 0/i);

  fireEvent.click(button);

  expect(screen.getByText(/count: 1/i)).toBeInTheDocument();
  expect(display).not.toBeInTheDocument();  // Line X
});

a) What does { name: /increment/i } do in getByRole? (1 pt)

b) Why would Line X pass after clicking the button, even though display was assigned before the click? (2 pts)

c) What Jest matcher is used on Line X, and what does it assert? (1 pt)

▸ Show Answer

a) The name option filters buttons by their accessible name (e.g. visible text, aria-label). The regex /increment/i matches case-insensitively — it finds a button whose accessible name contains "increment".

b) When the count changes from 0 to 1, React re-renders the component and the DOM node that contained "count: 0" is replaced — it is removed from the document. The variable display still holds a reference to the old, now-detached DOM node. So expect(display).not.toBeInTheDocument() passes because that specific element is no longer in the live DOM.

c) .toBeInTheDocument() — from @testing-library/jest-dom. It asserts that the element is present in the document body. Combined with .not, it asserts the element has been removed from the DOM.

Question 34 — Multiple Choice

You want to test that an element is not present in the DOM after an action. Which query/assertion combination is correct?

  • A) expect(screen.getByText('Error')).not.toBeInTheDocument()
  • B) expect(screen.queryByText('Error')).not.toBeInTheDocument()
  • C) expect(screen.findByText('Error')).not.toBeInTheDocument()
  • D) expect(screen.queryByText('Error')).toBeNull()
▸ Show Answer

B (or D — both are valid). Option A is wrong because getByText throws if the element is not found — the test would error before even reaching expect. Option C is wrong because findByText returns a Promise, not an element. Option B uses queryByText (returns null if absent) then asserts it is not in the document. Option D is also correct and slightly simpler. In practice, B is idiomatic RTL style.

SECTION J: SERVICE WORKERS & PWAs

Question 35 — Fill in the blanks

Complete the following sentences about Service Workers:

A Service Worker runs in a separate 1. ________ thread and has no direct access to the DOM. It acts as a programmable 2. ________ between the browser and the network. Service Workers must be served over 3. ________ (or localhost for development). The lifecycle event fired after a Service Worker is installed and ready to take control is 4. ________. To make a Service Worker take control of existing pages immediately without waiting for a reload, you call 5. ________ inside the activate event.

▸ Show Answer

1. worker (background thread) — no DOM access

2. network proxy / interceptor

3. HTTPS

4. activate

5. self.clients.claim()

Question 36 — Multiple Choice

In a Service Worker fetch event handler, which caching strategy serves the cached version immediately while also updating the cache in the background for future requests?

  • A) Cache First
  • B) Network First
  • C) Stale While Revalidate
  • D) Cache Only
▸ Show Answer

C — Stale While Revalidate: responds immediately with the cached (possibly stale) version for speed, then fetches a fresh version from the network in the background to update the cache for the next request. This is the best strategy for resources that should be fast to load but can tolerate slightly outdated data (e.g. fonts, non-critical assets).

Cache First serves from cache always, only going to network if not cached. Network First hits the network, falls back to cache only if offline. Cache Only never goes to network.

SECTION K: FORMDATA API

Question 37 — Multiple Choice

Which of the following correctly uses the FormData API to send a file upload via fetch?

  • A) fetch('/upload', { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'multipart/form-data' }, body: JSON.stringify(formData) })
  • B) fetch('/upload', { method: 'POST', body: formData })
  • C) fetch('/upload', { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: formData })
  • D) fetch('/upload', { method: 'GET', body: formData })
▸ Show Answer

B — When you pass a FormData object as the body, the browser automatically sets the correct Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=... header including the boundary string. You must NOT set the Content-Type manually because the boundary would be missing and the server could not parse the body. Option A JSON-stringifies the FormData (produces "{}"), option C sets the wrong content type, option D uses GET (GET requests cannot have a body).

Question 38 — Written Answer (3 pts)

Explain why you should NOT manually set Content-Type: multipart/form-data when sending a FormData object with fetch.

▸ Show Answer

The multipart/form-data content type requires a boundary parameter that separates the different parts of the request body (e.g. Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=----WebKitFormBoundaryXYZ). This boundary string is generated by the browser and embedded throughout the request body.

If you manually set Content-Type: multipart/form-data without the boundary, the header is malformed — the server cannot determine where each part starts and ends, causing parsing failures. By omitting the Content-Type header entirely when using FormData, you let the browser set the complete header including the correct boundary automatically.

SECTION L: DESIGN PATTERNS

Question 39 — Fill in the blanks

Match each design pattern to its description:

The 1. ________ pattern provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem. The 2. ________ pattern ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access to it. The 3. ________ pattern defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state, all dependents are notified automatically. The 4. ________ pattern attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically without altering its class. The 5. ________ pattern uses shared objects to efficiently support large numbers of fine-grained objects (shares intrinsic state). The 6. ________ pattern defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate.

▸ Show Answer

1. Facade

2. Singleton

3. Observer (Pub/Sub)

4. Decorator

5. Flyweight

6. Factory (Factory Method)

Question 40 — Multiple Choice

In JavaScript, the built-in EventEmitter (Node.js) and the browser's addEventListener are both examples of which design pattern?

  • A) Facade
  • B) Iterator
  • C) Mediator
  • D) Observer
▸ Show Answer

D — Observer. Both implement the Observer (Publish/Subscribe) pattern: a subject maintains a list of observers (event listeners) and notifies them when an event occurs. EventEmitter.on('event', handler) and element.addEventListener('click', handler) both register observers; emit / dispatching an event notifies them.

Question 41 — Written Answer (4 pts)

Explain the Mediator pattern. How does it differ from the Observer pattern? Give a concrete JavaScript/web example of each.

▸ Show Answer

Observer: Objects (observers) subscribe to a subject/emitter and react when the subject emits an event. The subject broadcasts to all subscribers — it does not know what each subscriber will do. Coupling is between each observer and the subject. Example: React's custom event bus (mitt) — a component publishes 'cartUpdated' and multiple other components subscribe independently.

Mediator: A central coordinator (mediator) handles all communication between components. Components do not communicate directly with each other — they send messages to the mediator and the mediator decides who to notify. This reduces the number of direct dependencies between components. Example: A Redux store or an RTK store.dispatch — UI components never call each other directly; they all dispatch to the store (mediator) which updates state and re-renders the correct components. A chat room server is also a classic mediator: users don't send messages to each other directly, they send to the server which routes messages.

Key difference: Observer = many objects watching one subject; Mediator = one coordinator managing communication between many objects.

Question 42 — Select All That Apply

Which of the following are correct statements about the Iterator pattern in JavaScript?

  • [ ] An object is iterable in JavaScript if it has a [Symbol.iterator] method that returns an iterator
  • [ ] An iterator must implement a next() method that returns { value, done }
  • [ ] Arrays, Sets, and Maps are all iterable by default in JavaScript
  • [ ] Plain objects ({}) are iterable by default
  • [ ] Generator functions (function*) automatically create iterators
  • [ ] for...of loops use the Iterator protocol internally
▸ Show Answer

Correct answers:

  • [x] Iterable requires [Symbol.iterator] returning an iterator
  • [x] Iterator next() returns { value, done }
  • [x] Arrays, Sets, Maps are iterable
  • [ ] Plain objects are iterable — FALSE. Plain objects do not have [Symbol.iterator] by default. You must add it manually or use Object.keys()/Object.entries() to iterate.
  • [x] Generator functions implement the iterator protocol automatically
  • [x] for...of calls [Symbol.iterator] internally

Question 43 — Code Analysis (4 pts)

Consider the following code that uses the Decorator pattern:

class Logger {
  log(message) {
    console.log(message);
  }
}

class TimestampDecorator {
  constructor(logger) {
    this.logger = logger;
  }
  log(message) {
    this.logger.log(`[${new Date().toISOString()}] ${message}`);
  }
}

class LevelDecorator {
  constructor(logger, level) {
    this.logger = logger;
    this.level = level;
  }
  log(message) {
    this.logger.log(`[${this.level}] ${message}`);
  }
}

const logger = new LevelDecorator(new TimestampDecorator(new Logger()), 'INFO');
logger.log('Server started');

a) What will be printed to the console? (2 pts)

b) Explain the advantage of this approach compared to adding timestamp and level functionality directly to the Logger class. (2 pts)

▸ Show Answer

a) [INFO] [2024-xx-xxTxx:xx:xx.xxxZ] Server started (with a real ISO timestamp). The call chain is: LevelDecorator.log prepends [INFO] and calls TimestampDecorator.log, which prepends a timestamp and calls Logger.log, which calls console.log.

b) The Decorator pattern allows composing behaviors dynamically without modifying the base class. Advantages:

  • Open/Closed Principle — the base Logger class is closed for modification but open for extension via decorators.
  • Mix and match — you can use TimestampDecorator alone, LevelDecorator alone, both together, or in any order, without creating a combinatorial explosion of subclasses.
  • Adding the behavior directly to Logger would bloat the class and violate Single Responsibility; it would also be impossible to use the logger without those features.

Question 44 — Written Answer (4 pts)

Explain the Flyweight pattern. Describe a concrete scenario in a web application where applying it would provide significant memory savings.

▸ Show Answer

Flyweight pattern: Instead of creating a new object for each similar entity, the pattern shares a single instance of the common (intrinsic) state across many objects. The unique (extrinsic) state is stored externally and passed in when needed.

Concrete web scenario: A mapping application renders 100,000 map markers, each representing a point of interest. Each marker has: a type (restaurant, hotel, park — intrinsic/shared) and its GPS coordinates + name (extrinsic/unique). Without Flyweight, 100,000 objects would each contain a copy of the icon, style, and behavior for their type. With Flyweight, only 3 shared "type" objects exist (Restaurant, Hotel, Park), and each marker holds only its coordinates/name plus a reference to the shared type. Memory usage drops dramatically.
